title: China Eximbank provides RMB 2.3 billion government concessional loan for Phase 2 of Nigeria National Information and Communication Technology Infrastructure Backbone (NICTIB) Project (Linked to Project ID#30340)
description: In November 2006, Galaxy Backbone Limited — a public enterprise of the Federal Government of Nigeria incorporated in 2006 with the primary mandate of setting up and operating a unified Information and Communication Technology (ICT) infrastructure platform that addresses the connectivity, transversal and other technology challenges — signed a $117 million commercial contract with Huawei Technologies Co. Ltd for Phase 1 of the Nigeria National Information and Communication Technology Infrastructure Backbone (NICTIB) Project.   Nearly four and a half years later, in May 2011, China Eximbank agreed in principle to provide a $100 million preferential buyer’s credit to finance 85% of the cost of the commercial contract. Then, on January 5, 2013, China Eximbank and the Government of Nigeria signed a $100,000,000 preferential buyer’s credit (PBC) agreement for Phase 1 of the Nigeria National Information and Communication Technology Infrastructure Backbone (NICTIB) Project (captured via Project ID#30340). The PBC carried the following borrowing terms: a 20 year maturity, a 7 year grace period, and a 2.5% interest rate. The final maturity date of the loan (PBC) is September 21, 2032. As of December 31, 2020, Nigeria’s Debt Management Office (DMO) reported that the China Eximbank PBC (loan) had achieved a 100% disbursement rate ($100 million) and the borrower had made interest repayments worth $10.66 million and principal repayments worth $3.86 million to the lender. The loan’s (principal) amount outstanding, as of December 31, 2020, was $96.14 million. Under the terms of the PBC agreement, the Government of Nigeria was responsible for paying 15% of the cost of the commercial contract with Huawei Technologies Co. Ltd prior to project implementation.   Advance payment delays by the Government of Nigeria resulted in major project implementation delays. LG3 is located in Lagos, Nigeria and will deliver new colocation capacity supporting Nigeria's strategic position as an international hub for subsea cable connections. The $100 million plan includes expansions at existing sites in Nigeria, Ghana, and Côte d'Ivoire, as well as a new facility in Port Harcourt (PR1) serving as a 2Africa cable landing station.

recip_org: Government of Cameroon |South Atlantic Inter Link Consortium
title: China Eximbank provides RMB 3.06 billion ‘mixed credit’ for 5900 km South Atlantic Inter Link (SAIL) Cable Project
description: On October 14, 2015, China Unicom and CAMTEL — a state-owned  national telecommunications and Internet service provider in Cameroon — signed a partnership agreement for the 5900 km South Atlantic Inter Link (SAIL) Cable Project. Then, on August 11, 2016, China Unicom and CAMTEL signed a Construction and Maintenance Agreement (C&MA) for the South Atlantic Inter Link (SAIL) Cable Project, which established a special purpose vehicle (SPV) called the South Atlantic Inter Link Consortium (南大西洋国际海底光缆组织). China Eximbank subsequently signed a loan agreement worth approximately RMB 3.06 billion with South Atlantic Inter Link Consortium (南大西洋国际海底光缆组织) — a joint venture between China Unicom and CAMTEL — for the 5900 km South Atlantic Inter Link (SAIL) Cable Project. The borrowing terms of the loan are unknown. However, it is known that the borrower was expected to use the proceeds of the loan to finance 85% of the total cost (RMB 3.6 billion) of the project (including a commercial contract with Huawei Marine Networks Co., Limited that was signed on June 27, 2017). The remainder (15%) of the project cost was to be financed by China Unicom, Huawei Marine Networks Co., Limited and CAMTEL.   The purpose of the project was to lay a 5900 km submarine internet cable -- called the South Atlantic Inter Link (SAIL) -- from Kribi, Cameroon to Fortaleza, Brazil. SAIL was expected to become the first intercontinental fast direct submarine cable spanning the South Atlantic Ocean and connecting the African and South American continents. The SAIL cable system was designed to be a fast and direct path, utilizing the industry leading 100G transmission technology and to deliver a design capacity of 32 Tbps, through a 4 fiber pair configuration. Huawei Marine Networks Co., Limited was the general EPC contractor responsible for implementation. However, China Unicom also provided project management and business operations support. The submarine fiber optic cable for the project was manufactured in
co_financiers: Cameroon Telecommunications (CAMTEL)|China United Network Communications Group Co., Ltd. (China Unicom) (CUniq)
notes: Recoded from CMR to REG (continent-to-continent capacity). SAIL is a 5,900 km intercontinental submarine cable from Kribi (Cameroon) to Fortaleza (Brazil); Cameroon is the sole African landing but the cable provides continental-scale capacity that benefits any African ISP buying transit. Treated analogously to other regional cable rows (2Africa, Equiano, East2West). Previous notes: Cross-verified: AidData RMB 3.06B loan (85% of project cost) confirmed by SubmarineNetworks.com, Wikipedia, multiple press sources. Funds support a build-to-suit strategy to develop new telecom towers for 3G/4G/5G deployment in the Democratic Republic of Congo, Nigeria, and Côte d'Ivoire. title: Africa Data Centres — $500 Million Pan-African Data Centre Build-Out Programme
description: In September 2021, Africa Data Centres (ADC), a subsidiary of Cassava Technologies (formerly Liquid Intelligent Technologies), announced a $500 million investment programme to build 10 interconnected, cloud- and carrier-neutral data centres across 10 African countries over two years, doubling its existing footprint. The programme is financed through a combination of debt and equity, including a $300 million DFC direct loan (committed 2020, first disbursement Aug 2022 for South Africa), corporate equity from Cassava Technologies/ADC, and local debt facilities. Target countries include South Africa (Johannesburg JHB1, JHB2, Cape Town CPT1, CPT2), Kenya (Nairobi NBO1, NBO2), Nigeria (Lagos LOS1), Ghana (Accra ACC1), Togo (Lomé), and others. As of 2024, ADC has spent the $500M and operates facilities from South Africa to West Africa.

recip_org: Governments of Kenya, Rwanda, Sierra Leone, Niger, Zimbabwe, Benin, Botswana, South Africa et al.
title: Giga — School Connectivity Initiative for Sub-Saharan Africa (UNICEF-ITU Joint Programme)
description: UNICEF-ITU joint initiative launched in 2019 to connect every school to the internet and every young person to information, opportunity and choice. In SSA, Giga is active in 20+ countries including Kenya, Rwanda, Sierra Leone, Niger, Zimbabwe, Benin, Botswana, Namibia, South Africa and Guinea, providing school mapping, connectivity planning, procurement support, and financing facilitation. By November 2023, Giga had mobilized >USD 47.6 million from 14 partners (Ericsson, Dubai Cares, Musk Foundation, SoftBank, BCG, IHS Towers, GSMA, Dell, FCDO, and others). UNICEF is the primary lead of the Giga initiative with ITU as co-lead. Activities include open-source school mapping (Project Connect), real-time connectivity monitoring, government procurement support, and innovative financing models including outcome bonds and connectivity credits. As of 2023, Giga had connected 14,500+ schools globally, benefiting 7.79 million students, with SSA countries (Kenya, Rwanda, Sierra Leone, Botswana) accounting for a significant share.
co_financiers: ITU; Ericsson; Dubai Cares; Musk Foundation; SoftBank; BCG; IHS Towers; GSMA; Dell; FCDO; Jumia; Mapbox; NIC.Br; Actual
notes: UNICEF is lead financier/programme owner of Giga. Disclosed partner contributions to the initiative total >USD 47.6M as of April 2023 (ITU TDAG30 document). Giga has additionally leveraged USD 1.7B+ in 8 countries for school connectivity from government USFs, loans, and auctions. Amount recorded is the directly raised partner contributions per disclosed figure. SSA countries include Kenya, Rwanda, Sierra Leone, Niger, Zimbabwe, Benin, Botswana, Namibia, South Africa, Guinea, Madagascar, Malawi (via Airtel/UNICEF Reimagine Education), Tanzania, Uganda. Giga Bond concept ($5B) was explored but not issued; pivoted to Giga Fund. Cross-ref: itu.csv for ITU's co-lead role and WTDC Resolution 87 implementation.

title: Visa $1 Billion Africa Digital Transformation Pledge (2022–2027) (2022 tranche)
description: During the US-Africa Business Forum in December 2022, Visa announced a pledge to invest $1 billion in Africa by 2027 to advance resilient, innovative and inclusive economies. The five-year commitment covers: scaling Visa operations across Africa (now 10 offices up from 1); deploying new payment innovations and technologies; deepening collaboration with governments, financial institutions, MNOs, fintechs, and merchants. Includes the Africa Fintech Accelerator, investments in African fintechs, and new data infrastructure. Countries include all 54 African nations; SSA represents the majority of operations.
notes: $1 billion commitment announced Dec 14, 2022 at US-Africa Business Forum, Washington DC. Multi-year 2022-2027. Components include fintech accelerator, equity in African fintechs, data centre infrastructure, skills/inclusion. REG used as commitment spans all 54 African countries; SSA comprises majority of operations. Sub-deals (Interswitch, Flutterwave, Moniepoint, Africa Fintech Accelerator, ZAF data centre) recorded separately where amounts are individually disclosed. | Original $1B 5-year Africa pledge split straight-line 2022-2026.

title: Maroc Telecom Transafrican Terrestrial Fibre Cable - Landlocked SSA Connectivity
description: Maroc Telecom invested in a 5,700 km transafrican terrestrial cable connecting Morocco to Niger via Mauritania, Mali, and Burkina Faso, as part of MAD 2 billion+ invested in transafrican connectivity infrastructure. This cable enables the landlocked Moov Africa subsidiaries in Mauritania, Mali, Burkina Faso, and Niger to connect to international bandwidth via the Maroc Telecom network. Investment disclosed as part of Maroc Telecom's 2024 reporting.
notes: LesEco states Maroc Telecom invested >MAD 2B in transafrican connectivity including the 5,700km terrestrial cable and the West Africa submarine cable. Amount-quality Approx as MAD amount for terrestrial cable alone not separately broken out from total >MAD 2B. Estimated at MAD 1B (~$100M) for terrestrial component. SSA countries: MRT, MLI, BFA, NER. Connects to submarine cable at CIV and TGO.

title: Liquid Intelligent Technologies USD 300 Million Eurobond — Balance Sheet Reset (2026)
description: In April 2026, Liquid Intelligent Technologies (a Cassava Technologies subsidiary) issued a USD 300 million Eurobond (listed on Euronext Dublin; Rule 144A/Reg S; 2.5x oversubscribed). Part of a broader USD 660M+ refinancing package including: USD 300M Eurobond, USD 210M ZAR syndicated term loan (Nedbank, RMB, Standard Bank, IFC) providing currency hedge on South African revenues, USD 150M USD syndicated term loan (Ninety One/EAAIF, MCB). Cassava simultaneously injected USD 195M fresh equity. DEG was anchor DFI investor in bond. Transaction extended debt maturity profile, reset balance sheet, and provides headroom for further digital infrastructure expansion across 25+ African countries.
co_financiers: ZAR term loan: Nedbank, Rand Merchant Bank, Standard Bank, IFC; USD term loan: Ninety One/EAAIF (PIDG), Mauritius Commercial Bank; DEG as anchor bond investor (amount not disclosed)
notes: SOURCE ROW: Liquid (Cassava subsidiary) 2026 bond issuance at parent level. co-finance-usd = USD 210M ZAR facility + USD 150M USD facility + USD 195M equity injection = USD 555M total (Cassava equity injection is from Cassava own resources, not external DFI). DEG anchor ticket not yet confirmed in deg.csv — verify if deg-reg-liquid-intelligent-technologies-bond-2026 row has been added to deg.csv by deg subagent. EAAIF/Ninety One USD 150M term loan participant (check pidg.csv for row). DO NOT duplicate ADC rows.

recip_org: Maroc Telecom / Moov Africa West Africa Cable Consortium
title: Maroc Telecom West Africa Submarine Fibre Optic Cable - €150M Investment
description: Maroc Telecom invested €150 million ($157 million) in the construction of the West Africa submarine fibre optic cable, a 9,414 km system connecting the group's Moov Africa SSA subsidiaries to the international network via Morocco. The cable has 8 landing points across 7 countries including Côte d'Ivoire (Abidjan), Togo (Lomé), Benin (Cotonou), Gabon (Libreville), Mauritania (Nouadhibou), Morocco (Casablanca, Dakhla), and Portugal (Lisbon). South segment operational July 2021; North segment operational April 2022. Capacity: 20-60 Tbps. Financed by a consortium of Maroc Telecom and its African subsidiaries.
co_financiers: Moov Africa CIV; Moov Africa TGO; Moov Africa BEN; Moov Africa GAB; Moov Mauritel
notes: €150M confirmed by Maroc Telecom at GITEX Africa 2023 and multiple sources. SSA landing countries: CIV, TGO, BEN, GAB, MRT. South segment (8,600 km, 20 Tbps, Casablanca to CIV/TGO/BEN/GAB/MRT) operational July 2021; North segment (Casablanca-Lisbon, 60Tbps) operational April 2022. Also connects landlocked subsidiaries in MLI, BFA, NER via terrestrial links. €150M converted at 1.047 EUR/USD (2021 avg) = ~$157M.

title: D4D Programme / WeHubit 1.0 — Digital Social Innovation Grants
description: Wehubit 1.0 (D4D Programme) implemented by Enabel and BIO on behalf of Belgian development cooperation. Supports scaling of digital social innovations (fintech, agritech, e-health, edtech, digital infrastructure) across 14 official Belgian partner countries in SSA. EUR 9.345M Enabel-managed budget; additional EUR 14M committed by BIO for private sector digitisation projects. Programme provided grants of EUR 50K–350K to 27+ digital social innovations across 13 Enabel partner countries.
co_financiers: BIO (EUR 14M for private sector digitisation)
notes: EUR/USD rate 2018: 1.181. Budget EUR 9.345M from IATI/open.enabel.be (total intervention EUR 10M per portal). BIO separately committed EUR 14M for private sector digitisation. Programme covered 13-14 SSA partner countries. IATI country code BE (Belgium as funder hub); actual implementation in SSA partner countries.

title: Wehubit 2.0 — Scaling Digital Social Innovation
description: Wehubit 2.0 builds on the Wehubit 1.0 programme (2018-2023) to continue identifying and supporting scaling of digital and green social innovations in the public and non-profit sectors. Programme is implemented globally but focused on Belgian partner countries in SSA. Budget EUR 4.67M financed by Belgium. Supports digital entrepreneurship, digital governance, and digital social innovation across SSA, with grants of EUR 100K–300K per innovation.
notes: EUR/USD rate 2023: 1.081. Budget EUR 4.672M from open.enabel.be. IATI country code BE; programme global but focused on SSA partner countries including Burundi, DRC, Mozambique, Uganda, Tanzania. Part of broader Digital and Green Innovation (DGI) action.

notes: EUR/USD rate 2023: 1.081. Budget EUR 2.253M from open.enabel.be. EU-funded. Programme code BEL22006. IATI country = BE (hub); programme primarily targets African countries per title "Data Governance in Africa".

title: Partech Africa II Fund LP Commitment

The fund invests initial tickets of $1M–$15M from Seed to Series C across African tech and digital companies covering fintech, healthtech, edtech, mobility, logistics and other digital sectors in Sub-Saharan Africa and pan-African markets. This is the first deployment under Bpifrance's €350M Africa co-investment partnership with Mubadala (signed June 2021). Bpifrance and Mubadala announced their joint commitment on 6 March 2024. Fund invests across 27+ African countries.

financier_name: British International Investment (BII, formerly CDC Group)

Investment 1 ($25M, Feb 2022): Bond investment to add 3 million new subscribers in Tanzania and expand coverage in other SSA markets. Investment 2 ($30M, Feb 2024): Facility to construct 200 new telecom sites across Tanzania (mainland and Zanzibar), increasing rural connectivity for up to 600,000 people. Axian also operates mobile money services (Mvola in Madagascar, T-PESA in Tanzania).
notes: Two investment tranches: F338201-01 $25M 2022-02-02, F353301-02 $30M 2024-02-01. Also F371501-01 $25M 2025-06-23 (newest tranche — recorded separately as bii-reg-004). Disbursed from parent F338201 aggregate $18.6M.

financier_name: British International Investment (BII, formerly CDC Group)
recip_org: Liquid Telecommunications Jersey Limited (Cassava Technologies data centre business)
title: Liquid Intelligent Technologies — Follow-on Equity (Data Centres)
description: Follow-on $40M equity investment by BII in Liquid Telecommunications Jersey Limited in July 2020, to enhance and expand Liquid's data centre business across Sub-Saharan Africa. Liquid Telecom is the largest independent data centre operator in Sub-Saharan Africa, operating facilities in Johannesburg, Nairobi, Harare, Lusaka and elsewhere. This investment supports cloud hosting, colocation services, and internet exchange points across SSA, directly enabling digital infrastructure for businesses.
notes: Separate IATI record from F322801. Zimbabwe-coded in IATI but description confirms pan-SSA data centre operations. Status=4 (Closed). BII evaluation report confirms: "$40M equity investment to enhance Liquid's data centre business" in 2020.

financier_name: British International Investment (BII, formerly CDC Group)
title: TerraPay — Debt Investment (Cross-Border Payments Africa)
description: BII provided a $20M senior secured loan to TerraPay in March 2024, a global cross-border payments processor focused on remittance transfers into Africa. BII financing is structured through Lendable's senior secured facility. The investment pre-funds increasing remittance volumes to Africa via key corridors: Kenya, Ghana, Uganda, Tanzania, Cameroon, Mali, Benin, Côte d'Ivoire, Senegal, Mozambique. TerraPay processes digital remittances lowering costs and increasing speed for African diaspora sending money home.
co_financiers: Lendable (facility manager); Prime Ventures; Partech
notes: F354201-01: $20M commit Mar 2024. IATI coded Kenya (TerraPay regional HQ). Operates across 10+ SSA countries — classified Regional. BII press release 2024 confirms $20M senior loan via Lendable.

financier_name: Belgian Investment Company for Developing Countries (BIO)

description: EUR 10M equity in AfricInvest Financial Inclusion Vehicle (FIVE), formerly AfricInvest Financial Sector Holding. An evergreen pan-African investment fund focused on financial inclusion across Africa. Initial strategy targeted established banks, insurers, and NBFIs; evolved to include fintech companies with digital financial services. BIO partner for 20+ years with AfricInvest, investing EUR 76M total across 9 AfricInvest funds.
co_financiers: AfricInvest, other DFI LPs
notes: EUR/USD rate 2017: 1.130. EUR 10M equity commitment. Fund launched 2017; BIO initial commitment at inception. Commit-year 2017. Pan-African with SSA focus. Digital inclusion through fintech investments in SSA financial institutions and fintechs.

financier_name: Belgian Investment Company for Developing Countries (BIO)
title: Cathay Africinvest Innovation Fund — Pan-African Venture Capital
description: EUR 6M (two tranches: EUR 4M in 2021 + EUR 2M follow-on) equity in Cathay AfricInvest Innovation Fund (CAIF), a pan-African VC fund. CAIF reached €110M final close in 2023, co-managed by AfricInvest and Cathay Innovation. Invests in digital tech companies from seed to growth stage across Africa (Egypt, Kenya, Nigeria, South Africa). Focus on digital economy, fintech, healthtech, edtech, and agritech startups with scaling potential.
co_financiers: EIB, Proparco, AfricInvest, Cathay Innovation (fund manager)
notes: EUR/USD rate 2021: 1.183. BIO portal shows EUR 6M total (two tranches: EUR 4M + EUR 2M both dated 21/05/2021). CAIF final close EUR 110M in Jan 2023. Portfolio countries: Egypt (MENA-excluded from SSA count), Kenya, Nigeria, South Africa. Recorded as REG for SSA component.

financier_name: Belgian Investment Company for Developing Countries (BIO)

Fund targets fibre networks, wireless broadband, data centres, 5G infrastructure, and digital service provider platforms. Portfolio countries include Ghana, Kenya, Nigeria, South Africa (DRC, Ethiopia, Côte d'Ivoire per DFC filing). Fund size ~USD 250M+ with co-investors including EIB, DFC, Finnfund.
co_financiers: EIB, US DFC (USD 25M), Finnfund, DEG
notes: EUR 8.83M equity commitment disclosed by BIO portal (equivalent USD 10M per BIO disclosure). Commit-year 2022 per BIO SDG report reference. Fund targets SSA digital infrastructure. DFC filing shows host countries include DRC, Ethiopia, CIV, Nigeria, Ghana, Kenya, South Africa.

financier_name: Belgian Investment Company for Developing Countries (BIO)
title: Lendable MSME Fintech Credit Fund — Equity Commitment

SSA beneficiary countries: Kenya, Nigeria, South Africa (also Liberia, Sierra Leone, Tanzania, Uganda per related investments).

financier_name: Belgian Investment Company for Developing Countries (BIO)
title: TerraPay Payment Services — Digital Payments Infrastructure Loan

Supports expansion of TerraPay's interoperable payment rails enabling affordable international remittances and mobile payments across Africa (Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, Kenya, Senegal, Tanzania, Uganda).

notes: USD 10M from BIO as part of USD 75M IFC-led syndication for TerraPay (March 2024). Beneficiary countries: Cameroon, CIV, Kenya, Senegal, Tanzania, Uganda (SSA portion of global TerraPay network). Recorded as REG. IFC press release: March 2024.

financier_name: Belgian Investment Company for Developing Countries (BIO)
title: TIDE Africa Fund (TLcom) — Tech Venture Capital

Fund reached USD 71M final close in February 2020. Invests across the VC cycle in fast-growth digital tech companies serving SSA markets. Co-investors include IFC and South Africa's Sango Capital. Portfolio companies include Andela, Twiga Foods, ULesson. Primary focus countries: Kenya, Nigeria.
co_financiers: IFC, Sango Capital, EIB
notes: EUR/USD rate 2020: 1.142. BIO portal shows EUR 4.37M (USD 5M per BIO disclosures). TLcom TIDE Africa Fund final close USD 71M in Feb 2020 (per TechCabal). BIO participating alongside IFC and Sango Capital.

financier_name: Mubadala Investment Company / Bpifrance Africa Co-Investment
title: Bpifrance-Mubadala Africa Co-Investment Partnership (Mubadala share)
description: Mubadala Investment Company's half of the EUR 350 million Africa co-investment partnership announced jointly with French public investment bank Bpifrance on 30 June 2021 at the 13th UAE-France Strategic Dialogue. The MoU commits both parties to deploy capital through fund and direct investments in high-growth African startups, SMEs and mid-cap companies across regional, multi-country and pan-African private equity and venture capital funds. Mubadala's indicative share is half (~EUR 175M) of the stated EUR 350M total objective; the split is not explicitly 50/50 in official sources but no other split has been disclosed. Coverage: pan-African SSA (multi-country). First investment: Partech Africa II (March 2024, see separate mubadala row).

title: Cassava Technologies — NVIDIA AI Factory Partnership: Africa's First AI Data Centres (2024 tranche)
description: In March–April 2025, Cassava Technologies announced Africa's first AI factory in partnership with NVIDIA. Cassava committed up to USD 720 million to deploy 12,000 NVIDIA GPUs across five regional AI data centre hubs over 3–4 years: South Africa (first phase, 3,000 GPUs live June–November 2025), Nigeria, Kenya, Egypt, and Morocco. The South Africa hub went live in November 2025. In October 2025, NVIDIA made an equity investment in Cassava (amount undisclosed) as part of a USD 150M+ fresh equity capital raise for Liquid Intelligent Technologies; STANLIB also agreed a minority stake in ADC. The NVIDIA investment supports GPU-as-a-Service commercial deployment. Cassava is an NVIDIA Cloud Partner (NCP). The initiative aims to enable sovereign AI capabilities, local model training, and AI skills development for 10 million Africans. Primary SSA countries: ZAF, NGA, KEN. Egypt and Morocco are non-SSA but SSA portion is primary focus.
co_financiers: NVIDIA Corporation (GPU supply + equity investment, amount undisclosed); STANLIB (ADC minority stake, amount undisclosed)
notes: USD 720M is Cassava's own multi-year CapEx commitment (Bloomberg April 2025; multiple sources). NVIDIA equity investment in Cassava undisclosed amount per Oct 2025 Cassava press release. DO NOT duplicate ADC data centre rows in adc.csv — this row captures Cassava parent-level AI factory commitment, including GPU procurement and AI-ready data centre upgrades. Multi-year 3–4 year window explicitly named: 2025–2028. Amount-quality = 'Multi-year aggregate' as USD 720M is total commitment over plan period. SSA countries: ZAF (primary hub), NGA, KEN. recip-iso3=REG per multi-country rule. | Original $720m 4-year AI factory programme split straight-line 2024-2027.

title: Cassava Technologies USD 90 Million Equity Round (DFC + Google + Finnfund, Dec 2024)
description: In December 2024, Cassava Technologies raised USD 90 million in equity as part of a broader USD 310 million financing package. The equity was led by the US International Development Finance Corporation (DFC, USD 50M), Google LLC (USD 20M approx.), and Finnfund (undisclosed). Alongside this, Liquid Intelligent Technologies (Cassava subsidiary) refinanced its South African Rand term loan (~USD 220M equivalent) with Standard Bank, Rand Merchant Bank, Nedbank, and IFC. Cassava simultaneously completed a legal reorganisation to create an integrated digital solutions platform spanning broadband, data centres, cloud, cybersecurity, AI compute, and payments across 30+ countries. Cassava operates fibre networks, Africa Data Centres (ADC) data centres, Liquid C2 cloud/cybersecurity, and GPU-as-a-Service.
co_financiers: US DFC (USD 50M — see dfc.csv dfc-reg-cassava-technologies-2024); Google LLC (USD ~20M); Finnfund; South African Rand refinancing: Standard Bank, Rand Merchant Bank, Nedbank, IFC
notes: Records Cassava's GROUP-LEVEL equity round (parent level). DO NOT duplicate ADC data centre builds — those are in adc.csv. DFC USD 50M ticket already in dfc.csv (dfc-reg-cassava-technologies-2024). Google USD ~20M figure per TechCrunch; exact split not disclosed. Finnfund amount undisclosed. Total equity = USD 90M confirmed by DFC and Cassava. IFC involvement limited to ZAR refinancing (debt, not equity in this round). BII, Royal Bafokeng Holdings, PIC, Gateway Capital, Afreximbank/FEDA are pre-existing shareholders (earlier rounds).

title: CPDIF $10M Growth Investment in 42Markets Group — Capital Markets Fintech Platform
description: Convergence Partners Digital Infrastructure Fund (CPDIF) acquired a strategic stake in 42Markets Group for $10 million in growth funding. 42Markets Group is an incubator, investor and builder of high-growth specialist businesses in the financial and capital markets sector across Africa. The company focuses on digital transformation and improved efficiency of capital markets in Africa, enabling second-tier capital markets to reduce friction and costs, improve transparency and real-time risk management across financial products, ultimately extending the reach of banking and financial services to a broader cross-section of the population. Operations span South Africa, Nigeria, Kenya, Ghana, and Mauritius.
notes: Convergence Partners press release 24 April 2023 confirms $10M CPDIF strategic stake in 42Markets Group. Finnfund CPDIF portfolio page confirms '42Markets Group Holding Limited' as CPDIF portfolio company (countries: South Africa, Kenya, Mauritius, Ghana, Nigeria). All named SSA countries qualify. Non-SSA operations (UK, EU, Australia, India) secondary. Recorded as REG. flag-digital-payments=TRUE: primary purpose is digital transformation of financial markets, payment infrastructure, real-time risk management for capital markets. BII CPDIF Investment 01 portfolio confirms 42Markets Group Holding Ltd, June 2023, South Africa. CEO Brandon Doyle quote: 'one of our first investments in the digital transformation of financial markets.'

title: Digital Frontiers - USAID Center for Digital Development
description: Digital Frontiers is a $74.4 million seven-year (2017-2024) USAID cooperative agreement administered by DAI that works with USAID Bureaus and Missions to identify and scale digital approaches globally including Africa. Covers digital inclusion, fintech, ICT policy, DCCP, and digital health.
notes: Digital Frontiers $74.4M ceiling cooperative agreement. SSA activities include DCCP, digital inclusion, fintech programs. Amount is total ceiling; SSA attribution ~50%. amount-quality=Rounded

recip_org: Helios Towers PLC / HTA Group Ltd
title: DEG Anchor Investment in Helios Towers USD 750M Senior Bond (2020)
description: DEG acts as anchor investor in the USD 750 million senior bond issued by HTA Group Ltd, a subsidiary of Helios Towers plc, Africa's leading independent telecommunications tower company. The bond, the second in Helios Towers' corporate history, was one of the first capital market issuances by a private-sector company from a developing country since the COVID-19 pandemic outbreak. Helios Towers operates over 7,000 telecommunications towers in Tanzania, the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), Ghana, Congo Brazzaville, and South Africa, serving multiple mobile network operators. Proceeds used for tower expansion and operational growth.
co_financiers: Emerging Africa Infrastructure Fund/EAIF (USD 30M); IFC; private investors
notes: DEG confirmed as anchor investor in Helios Towers USD 750M bond (press release 2020-06-25). DEG-specific amount not publicly stated. USD 30,000,000 is an estimate based on typical DFI anchor participation (EAIF confirmed at USD 30M for same bond). Helios Towers SSA countries at time of issue: Tanzania, DRC, Ghana, Congo Brazzaville, South Africa. Bond matured June 2025. Multi-country → REG with country list: TZA, COD, GHA, COG, ZAF.

recip_org: Liquid Telecommunications Holdings / Liquid Intelligent Technologies
title: DEG Anchor Investment in Liquid Telecommunications USD 620M Bond (2021)
description: DEG invests USD 25 million as anchor investor in the USD 620 million bond issued by Liquid Telecommunications Financing PLC, part of the pan-African telecoms group Liquid Intelligent Technologies (owned by Econet Global). The bond was the first Liquid Telecom capital market issuance since 2017 and was oversubscribed 5x. Liquid Telecommunications operates Africa's largest multi-country fibre optic network across Sub-Saharan Africa including South Africa, Zimbabwe, Zambia, Kenya, Tanzania, DRC, and Botswana, also operating Africa Data Centres (ADC) as a subsidiary. Proceeds used to refinance existing debt and fund growth including new fibre infrastructure, data centres, and new technologies.
co_financiers: IFC (USD 100M); Emerging Africa Infrastructure Fund/EAIF (USD 53M); private investors
notes: USD 25,000,000 anchor investment confirmed from DEG press release 2021-03-10. Bond volume USD 620M. Liquid Telecommunications is pan-African fibre optic network operator and data centre business (ADC subsidiary). SSA countries: ZAF, ZWE, ZMB, KEN, TZA, COD, BWA and others. REG used for multi-country. Co-finance estimates: IFC confirmed USD 100M (16% of USD 620M), EAIF also participated. Note: 2026 Liquid Intelligent Technologies new USD 300M bond also includes DEG as anchor investor (April 2026) - separate deal not yet verified for amount.

title: Liquid Intelligent Technologies USD 620 Million Bond — Fibre and Data Centre Expansion (2021)
description: In February 2021, Liquid Telecommunications Financing PLC (a subsidiary of Liquid Intelligent Technologies, itself owned by Cassava Technologies) issued a USD 620 million bond oversubscribed 5x. Bond proceeds used to refinance existing debt and fund new fibre infrastructure, data centres, and new technologies across Liquid's 14+ SSA country network (ZAF, ZWE, ZMB, KEN, TZA, COD, UGA, RWA, BWA, ETH, MOZ, LSO, SSD, plus cross-border links). Liquid's network at issuance spanned approximately 100,000 km of fibre. DFI anchor investors: IFC USD 150M (bond) + USD 90M (equity) + USD 100M (ZAF term loan), DEG USD 25M, EAIF/PIDG USD 9.77M. This records the Cassava/Liquid corporate issuance.

title: DEG Anchor Investment in AXIAN Telecom USD 420M Bond (2022)
description: DEG invests USD 23 million as anchor investor in AXIAN Telecom's debut USD 420 million bond (7.375% senior notes due 2027). AXIAN Telecom is a pan-African telecommunications group operating in Tanzania (including Tigo Tanzania acquisition), Madagascar, Togo, Senegal, and Uganda. The bond proceeds are used to install approximately 1,250 new mobile-phone antennas, improve digital infrastructure and liquidity for future investments. Four DFI anchor investors: CDC Group (USD 30M), DEG (USD 23M), Emerging Africa Infrastructure Fund (EAIF), and IFC (USD 50M).
co_financiers: CDC Group/BII (USD 30M); IFC (USD 50M); Emerging Africa Infrastructure Fund (EAIF); private investors
notes: USD 23,000,000 anchor investment confirmed from DEG press release 2022-02-25. Total bond USD 420M. AXIAN Telecom SSA countries: TZA, MDG, TGO, SEN, UGA (all SSA-49). REG used for multi-country. CDC confirmed at USD 30M, IFC at USD 50M (12% of USD 420M per IFC press release). Bond matures 2027.

title: DEG Loan to IPT Powertech for Telecom Tower Energy in Africa
description: DEG provides a long-term loan of USD 25 million to IPT Powertech Group SAL (IPT), a Lebanon-based energy system integrator providing decentralised energy solutions for mobile network operators in Africa and the Middle East. IPT operates more than 30,000 towers in 11 countries in Africa and the Middle East. The loan enables investment in energy solutions to ensure telecom towers remain operational, supporting mobile connectivity across Sub-Saharan Africa including Nigeria, Ghana, and other markets. Co-investors include Proparco (USD 20M), Finnfund (USD 10M), and European Investment Bank (USD 60M).
co_financiers: Proparco (USD 20M); Finnfund (USD 10M); European Investment Bank/EIB (USD 60M)
notes: USD 25,000,000 loan confirmed from DEG press release 2019-10-02. IPT Powertech operates in Africa and Middle East with SSA countries including Nigeria, Ghana, Guinea, Sierra Leone per public sources. REG used as DEG investment covers multiple SSA countries. Co-finance: Proparco USD 20M + Finnfund USD 10M + EIB USD 60M = USD 90M. IPT description: energy solutions for telecom towers enabling mobile connectivity across SSA and MENA.

title: DEG Equity Investment in Copia Global E-Commerce and Fintech (2022)
description: DEG provides USD 7.5 million in equity capital to Copia Global Inc., a pan-African e-commerce company operating in Kenya and Uganda. Copia enables low- and middle-income consumers in East Africa to access a wide range of products through a network of agents using a digital platform. Over the past year, Copia expanded to include integrated FinTech services such as operating loans for Copia Agents, managed through a digital channel. Proceeds grow core business, fund pan-African expansion, and develop integrated financial services. Total round approximately USD 50 million. Co-investors include DFC, Goodwell Investments, Lightrock, and Zebu Investment Partners.
co_financiers: DFC; Goodwell Investments; Lightrock; Zebu Investment Partners
cat_primary_name: E-commerce, Private Sector Platforms, and Cross-Border Digital Trade
notes: USD 7,500,000 equity confirmed from DEG press release 2022-01-21. Copia operates in Kenya and Uganda (both SSA-49). REG used for multi-country. Total round ~USD 50M. recip_org: Partech Africa Fund II (PAF II) / Partech Partners
title: DEG Investment in Partech Africa Fund II (2023)
description: DEG acquires a EUR 15 million stake in Partech Africa Fund II (PAF II), the largest African VC fund focusing on technology companies including HealthTech, FinTech, and RetailTech. The fund concentrates investments in fast-growing technology companies across Africa, including Nigeria, Egypt, South Africa, Kenya, and Senegal. DEG supports access to long-term financing for young African technology companies. The investment adds Partech as the first African fund in DEG's global tech fund portfolio.
notes: EUR 15,000,000 confirmed from DEG press release 2023-02-08. FX: ECB annual average EUR/USD 2023 = 1.0813. USD = 15,000,000 * 1.0813 = 16,219,500 rounded to 16,220,000. Fund invests in Nigeria, Egypt, South Africa, Kenya, Senegal (multi-country → REG). Non-SSA countries (Egypt) included in fund scope but SSA dominates per fund description.

financier_name: DFAT (Australian Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade)
recip_org: Digital Earth Africa / Geoscience Australia / UNECA
title: Digital Earth Africa Phase II
description: Phase II of Digital Earth Africa expands the African Regional Data Cube (ARDC) pilot into continent-wide satellite remote sensing data infrastructure for 50+ African countries. Converts 30 years of satellite data into ready-to-use Earth observation products covering flooding, drought, soil and coastal erosion, agriculture, forest cover, land use, water availability and quality, and changes to human settlements. Hosted by UNECA; implemented by Geoscience Australia. Provides free, open data products with no cost to African users.
co_financiers: Helmsley Charitable Trust (USD 10,167,438)
cat_primary_name: Remote Sensing or Earth Observation Products or Services
notes: Commit AUD 10,000,000 confirmed from DFAT 2022-23 Africa Development Program Progress Report and independent evaluation. IATI (AU-5-INN133-298) shows disbursements only (5,500,000 AUD: 3,500,000 on 2019-06-30 + 2,000,000 on 2020-06-30) — no commit transaction recorded in IATI. FX: IMF annual average AUD/USD 2019 = 0.6953. Co-finance from Helmsley Charitable Trust (USD 10,167,438 per independent evaluation). Region 298 (Africa, all countries) = REG. Activity start date 2019-01-02. disbursed-usd = 5,500,000 × 0.6953 = 3,823,150.

title: Cassava Technologies — DFC $50M Strategic Equity Investment
description: DFC direct equity investment of $50 million in Cassava Technologies Ltd., one of Africa's largest pan-African digital infrastructure and services platforms. Cassava operates over 110,000 km of fibre optic networks across 30 countries, data centres, cloud services, cybersecurity, and AI tools. DFC led the equity round alongside Google LLC and Finnfund. Investment supports data centre expansion in Kenya, Ghana, Nigeria (and Morocco), providing directed use of proceeds for new data centre construction. Counters risks from strategic competitor digital ecosystems. Cassava equity round total $90M.
co_financiers: Google LLC ($20M approx.), Finnfund
notes: DFC FY2024 per annual data. Total equity round $90M: DFC $50M + Google + Finnfund. Cassava press Dec 12, 2024. DFC led the round. EWS data shows countries: Ghana, Kenya, Morocco, Nigeria. Morocco is non-SSA but majority of Cassava's operations are SSA. Recorded as REG. Note: later (Feb 2025) NVIDIA also invested in Cassava separately. DFC equity = $50M only (DFC portion of $90M round).

recip_org: Africa Data Centres Holdings Limited / Cassava Technologies
title: Africa Data Centres — DFC $300M Data Centre Loan
description: DFC direct loan of up to $300 million to Africa Data Centres Holdings Limited under a master finance agreement to finance the acquisition and expansion of existing data center assets in South Africa and Kenya, and entry into new markets through development, construction, and operation of carrier-neutral data centers across DFC-eligible African countries. First disbursement of $83M announced August 2022 primarily for South Africa expansion. Africa Data Centres is Africa's largest network of interconnected data facilities. Total project cost $428.6M. Supports seven existing and greenfield data centers in five countries.
co_financiers: Other DFIs (all-source $428.6M total)
notes: DFC investment story says 'In 2020, DFC approved financing of up to $300M'. DFC Annual Report FY2021 also lists it (FY2021 = Oct 2020-Sep 2021). Commit-year = 2020 per DFC narrative. Public Info Summary 9000093563. First disbursement $83M Aug 2022 for South Africa. Recipient countries: ZAF, KEN, and other eligible; recorded as REG per multi-country rule. Africa Data Centres is a Cassava Technologies / Econet Group business. PGII-linked investment.

title: Norrsken22 Africa Tech Growth Fund — DFC $15M Equity
description: DFC equity investment of up to $15 million in Norrsken22 Africa Tech Growth Fund, a women-led pan-African growth-stage technology fund investing in fintech, edtech, health tech, and market-enabling solutions across sub-Saharan Africa. Fund achieved final close at $205M. Portfolio companies include TymeBank (digital challenger bank), Smile Identity (identity verification), Autochek (auto financing), Sabi (B2B commerce platform), Shara (merchant financing). DFC is an anchor impact investor.
co_financiers: BII, IFC, Standard Bank, Norfund, SEB Pension Foundation, family offices
notes: DFC FY2022 per annual data. $15M equity investment announced Jun 2022. Final close $205M Nov 2023. Portfolio includes Smile Identity (digital ID verification), TymeBank (digital payments). flag-digital-payments=TRUE (TymeBank = digital bank). DFC share $15M only of $205M fund.

recip_org: Janngo Capital Startup Fund SLP / Janngo Capital Partners SAS
title: Janngo Capital Startup Fund — DFC $10M Equity
description: DFC equity investment of up to $10 million in Janngo Capital Startup Fund SLP, Africa's largest gender-equal tech VC fund, investing in early-stage innovative tech startups primarily in Francophone West Africa (Côte d'Ivoire, Senegal). At least 50% of portfolio companies are women-led or co-led. Fund targets health, education, food, financial services, mobility, and logistics tech startups. Final close at €78M (€$85M). Fund leverages technology to improve access to essential goods and services.
co_financiers: IFC, Mastercard Foundation Africa Growth Fund, ANAVA, Ashesi University, European DFIs
notes: DFC FY2023, $10M equity. DFC press release Oct 2023: 'Investing in tech startups in West Africa: A $10M equity investment in Janngo Capital Startup Fund SLP will increase funding for innovative tech startups in Africa.' Fund final close €78M (~$85M) Oct 2024. Primary markets CIV, SEN (SSA). DFC share $10M only of $85M total.

title: Africell Holding — OPIC $100M Telecom Expansion Loan
description: OPIC loan of $100 million to Africell Holding Limited to finance upgrades and expansion of 2G, 3G, and 4G mobile networks in The Gambia, Sierra Leone, Uganda, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo. Up to $40M may refinance existing debt. OPIC commitment letter signed November 2018; finance agreement concluded May 2019. Africell is a US-owned mobile operator and the first to receive DFC/OPIC financing. The facility supported over 12 million subscribers and provided affordable mobile voice and data services in frontier markets. Africell repaid the loan three years ahead of schedule in December 2024.
co_financiers: Gemcorp Capital, Gramercy Funds Management, TC Credit Partners, Helios Investment Partners (2021 commercial facility)
notes: OPIC commitment letter signed Nov 9, 2018 (pre-Jan 2019 = OPIC deal). Finance agreement signed May 2019. Countries: GMB, SLE, UGA, COD. DFC annual data shows FY2018. Project #9000042686. Loan 8-year tenor, 2-year grace period. Repaid 3 years early Dec 2024. DFC later amended facility to support Angola launch. OPIC instrument recorded as 'COPS IG' in DFC data = Comprehensive Overseas Private Investment Corporation Guaranty.

recip_org: Lendable Inc. / Lendable Fintech Credit Fund
title: Lendable MSME Fintech Credit Fund — DFC $20M Loan
description: DFC direct loan of up to $20 million to Lendable Fintech Credit Fund (Luxembourg RAIF), providing senior-secured debt facilities to early and mid-stage fintech companies operating in Africa (Kenya, Uganda, Nigeria, Ghana, South Africa, Senegal) and Asia. Lendable's data-driven approach enables on-lending to digital financial service providers serving underbanked MSMEs. DFC is an anchor investor. Total fund $110M. Promotes financial inclusion through digital lending platforms.
co_financiers: EMIIF (DFAT), Calvert Impact, Ceniarth, BIO, FMO, FSD Africa (FSDAi), SIFEM, OeEB
notes: DFC FY2021, $20M anchor investment. Lendable MSME Fintech Credit Fund soft close $49M Oct 2021; final close $110M 2022. DFC press release Sep 2021. Focus: Kenya, Uganda, Nigeria, Ghana, South Africa, Senegal. flag-digital-payments=TRUE as Lendable funds digital payment/lending fintechs. Classified 250600 DPI as fintech payments rail enabler. Total fund $110M; DFC share $20M only.

recip_org: Convergence Partners Digital Infrastructure Fund L.P. / Convergence Partners Africa
title: Convergence Partners Digital Infrastructure Fund — DFC $25M Equity
description: DFC equity investment of up to $25 million in Convergence Partners Digital Infrastructure Fund (CPDIF), a specialist Africa-focused ICT private equity fund. The fund invests in optical fibre installations, wireless towers, data centres, cloud, IoT, and AI infrastructure across sub-Saharan Africa. First close July 2021 at $120M; final close $296M. Portfolio companies include CSquared (Kenya broadband). DFC share is up to $25M out of $296M fund. Target countries: DRC, Ethiopia, Côte d'Ivoire, Nigeria, Ghana, Kenya, South Africa.
co_financiers: IFC ($25M), BII ($30M), Finnfund ($15M), EIB ($17.5M), other DFIs and institutional investors
notes: DFC equity up to $25M per Public Info Summary 9000104706. Fund target $300M; final close $296M Jan 2023. DFC FY2021 per annual report. Portfolio: CSquared (Kenya broadband), plus fibre, towers, data centres across SSA. IFC invested $25M separately. co-financiers include BII ($30M), Finnfund ($15M), EIB ($17.5M). DFC share = $25M only (DFC equity attribution rule).

recip_org: African Infrastructure Investment Fund 4 (AIIF4) / African Infrastructure Investment Managers (AIIM)
title: African Infrastructure Investment Fund 4 — DFC $50M Equity
description: DFC equity investment of up to $50 million in African Infrastructure Investment Fund 4 (AIIF4), a closed-end infrastructure fund managed by AIIM (African Infrastructure Investment Managers) with a target of $748M. Fund invests in digital infrastructure, energy transition, and mobility/logistics across Africa. Digital component includes telecom towers (Eastcastle Infrastructure, DRC and Nigeria, wireless telecom services). Fund achieved hard cap $748M with $954M including co-investments. DFC investment supports expansion of telecommunications and digital infrastructure across Sub-Saharan Africa.
co_financiers: IFC ($30M), FMO ($50M), DEG, Proparco, FinDev Canada ($26M), EIB, pension funds, institutional investors
notes: DFC FY2023, $50M equity. AIIF4 target $500-750M; achieved $748M. DFC press release Oct 2023. Eastcastle Infrastructure (DRC, Nigeria, wireless telecom towers) is confirmed digital portfolio company. Other SSA countries include CIV, KEN, ZAF. Non-SSA: EGY, MAR (Morocco) - fund invests across Africa including some North Africa but classified REG since it includes SSA countries. Note: Morocco classified non-SSA but included in DFC deal scope for SSA-eligible countries.

recip_org: ECP Africa Fund IV A LLC / Emerging Capital Partners
title: ECP Africa Fund IV — OPIC Direct Investment
description: OPIC direct investment of $100 million in ECP Africa Fund IV A LLC, a private equity fund managed by Emerging Capital Partners (ECP) investing in high-growth companies serving African consumers across multiple sectors. The fund includes IHS Holding Ltd (Nigeria, telecom tower infrastructure) among its portfolio companies. OPIC commitment in 2018 as part of the Connect Africa initiative to support technology and infrastructure across Sub-Saharan Africa.
co_financiers: Proparco, IFC, DEG
notes: OPIC FY2018 per DFC annual data. $100M DI. ECP Africa Fund IV contains IHS Holding (Nigeria telecom towers) as portfolio company per DFC FY24 subprojects. Total fund exposure ~$86.4M as of last report. OPIC/DFC instrument recorded as 'Direct Lending' in DFC data. Fund holds mixed portfolio; digital component via IHS Towers. Classified 250100 due to primary digital holding being IHS tower infrastructure.

title: DPI investment in Channel VAS mobile fintech platform — pan-African
description: Development Partners International (DPI), via its African Development Partners II/III fund, invests USD 54 million in Channel VAS (rebranded Optasia in 2022), the leading fintech provider of mobile financial and airtime credit services operating in 30+ African countries, the Middle East, and Asia. Channel VAS provides AI-based mobile financial services, nano loans, airtime credit and big data analytics through partnerships with mobile network operators and financial institutions. Over 85% of revenue derived from Sub-Saharan Africa. Convergence Partners co-invested.
co_financiers: Convergence Partners
notes: Channel VAS headquartered in Cyprus/Luxembourg but primary SSA operations. Operates in 30+ countries with 85%+ revenue from SSA. DPI ADP III fund. Co-investor: Convergence Partners. Channel VAS rebranded to Optasia in February 2022. DPI holds 25% initially through ADP II + ADP III. Amount confirmed at USD 54M in DPI press release. recip-iso3=REG as company operates pan-SSA.

financier_name: PIDG — Emerging Africa Infrastructure Fund (EAIF) / GuarantCo
title: WIOCC Corporate Finance Facility - EAIF
description: EAIF loaned USD 40 million (10-year term) as part of a USD 100 million debt package raised by the West Indian Ocean Cable Company (WIOCC). Proparco provided the remaining USD 60 million. WIOCC is a pan-African internet infrastructure provider operating fibre, cable systems, and data centres across Eastern and Southern Africa. Capital spending of at least USD 379 million planned across Eastern and Southern Africa.
co_financiers: Proparco (USD 60M)
notes: EAIF commit: USD 40M type-2 transaction 2020-09-30. Total package: USD 100M (EAIF $40M + Proparco $60M). Activity start 2020-07-11, planned end 2030-05-15. No recipient country in IATI -> REG (WIOCC is East/Southern Africa internet backbone). Also confirmed via press release.

financier_name: PIDG — Emerging Africa Infrastructure Fund (EAIF) / GuarantCo
title: IHS Towers Bond - EAIF Co-Anchor Investment
description: EAIF acted as co-anchor investor alongside IFC and Proparco in IHS Holding Limited's USD 1.2 billion bond issuance (oversubscribed at $2.3B). Proceeds refinance existing debt and support organic growth in African subsidiaries operating in Cameroon, Cote d'Ivoire, Nigeria, Rwanda, South Africa, and Zambia. IHS Towers is a leading independent telecom tower company with over 40,000 towers in Africa.
co_financiers: IFC; Proparco (co-anchor investors in USD 1.2B bond)
notes: IHS bond announced 2 December 2024. EAIF commit: USD 30M from IATI type-2 transaction 2024-12-31. Bond total: USD 1.2B. Countries: CMR, CIV, NGA, RWA, ZAF, ZMB (all SSA) -> REG. Note: separate from HTA bond entry.

title: Africell Holding Limited — USD 300 Million Bond (Mobile Network Operator SSA)
description: DFI participation in Africell Holding Limited's debut USD 300 million public senior secured bond (10.5% coupon, due 2029). Africell operates mobile networks in DRC, Sierra Leone, Uganda, and Gambia. Bond proceeds refinanced existing capital structure and fund network expansion. EAIF participated as sole impact investor (USD 28M). Proparco and IFC also participated as DFI anchor investors. Proparco's specific allocation not publicly disclosed.
co_financiers: EAIF/PIDG — USD 28M; IFC; private institutional investors — total USD 300M issuance
notes: Africell USD 300M bond (Oct 2024). EAIF press release confirms Proparco as co-anchor with IFC; specific Proparco ticket not disclosed. USD 15M estimated (Proparco is smaller DFI compared to EAIF USD 28M; using conservative estimate). Amount-quality=Estimated. Africell operates in COD, SLE, UGA, GMB — all SSA. REG. No AFD afd.csv overlap.

financier_name: PIDG — Emerging Africa Infrastructure Fund (EAIF) / GuarantCo
title: Africell USD 300M Bond - EAIF Sole Impact Investor
description: EAIF acted as sole impact investor anchoring Africell's debut USD 300 million international public bond (oversubscribed at $550M+). The financing supports roll-out of digital infrastructure across Angola, DRC, Gambia and Sierra Leone, enhancing connectivity for Africell's 14 million+ customers. EAIF committed USD 28 million (sole DFI/impact investor in the bond).
co_financiers: International institutional investors (USD 300M bond)
notes: Africell bond announced 29 October 2024. EAIF commit: USD 28M type-2 transaction 2024-12-31. Countries: AGO (Angola), COD (DRC), GMB (Gambia), SLE (Sierra Leone) -> REG. All SSA. cat-add-codes 251100 (Digital For Fragile And Conflict-Affected Situations) added as Gambia, DRC, Sierra Leone are FCS contexts.

financier_name: PIDG — Emerging Africa Infrastructure Fund (EAIF) / GuarantCo
title: HTA Group Ltd (Helios Towers Africa) Bond - EAIF
description: EAIF acted as anchor investor in bond issuances by Helios Towers Africa (HTA Group Ltd), a pan-African tower company owning and operating over 7,000+ telecom towers. The 2020 bond raised USD 750 million (EAIF: USD 30 million) to fund tower expansion in Tanzania, DRC, Congo Brazzaville and Ghana. EAIF made an additional USD 25 million commitment in 2024 as co-anchor investor alongside IFC and Proparco in the 1.2 billion bond alongside IHS Towers.
co_financiers: IFC; Proparco; DEG; Ninety One (2020 bond lead $750M; 2024 bond $1.2B)
notes: Two commit transactions in single IATI activity XM-DAC-47086-1D753128-EAIF: USD 30M (2020-12-31) + USD 25M (2024-12-31) = USD 55M total. Countries: Tanzania, DRC, Congo Brazzaville, Ghana (recip-iso3=REG). 2024 tranche part of $1.2B bond alongside IHS (see iati-project-id XM-DAC-47086-1AD4B491-EAIF). HTA operates ~15,000 towers across 9 countries in Africa.

financier_name: PIDG — Emerging Africa Infrastructure Fund (EAIF) / GuarantCo
title: Axian Telecom Bond - EAIF Anchor Investment
description: EAIF was an anchor investor in bond issuances by Axian Telecom, a pan-African telecommunications and digital company. First investment: USD 20M in the February 2022 bond (total USD 420M raised) to fund telecom expansion. Second tranche: USD 30M commitment in 2023 bond. Axian operates in Madagascar, Togo, Tanzania, Uganda, Senegal, Comoros and other markets.
co_financiers: Institutional investors (oversubscribed bond; USD 420M raised)
notes: Two commit transactions in IATI activity XM-DAC-47086-1D5EF955-EAIF: USD 20M (2022-12-31) + USD 30M (2023-12-31) = USD 50M total. First bond: Feb 2022 total USD 420M (EAIF scaled back to $20M from up to $40M due to private sector demand). Countries: MDG, TGO, TZA, UGA, SEN, COM -> REG.

financier_name: PIDG — Emerging Africa Infrastructure Fund (EAIF) / GuarantCo
title: Liquid Telecom Bond - EAIF Anchor Investment
description: EAIF anchored the bond issuance of Liquid Telecom to fund expansion of its pan-African telecom business. Liquid Telecom (now Liquid Intelligent Technologies) is a major fibre, cloud and broadband provider across 13+ African countries including DRC, Zambia, Zimbabwe, South Africa, Kenya, Uganda, Rwanda, Tanzania, Ethiopia, Mozambique, Botswana, Lesotho and South Sudan.
co_financiers: Institutional investors (bond market)
notes: Single commit type-2 transaction: USD 9,766,867 (2021-12-31). No recipient country in IATI -> REG (pan-African fibre/broadband operator). Activity start 2021-02-19, planned end 2026-09-04.

financier_name: PIDG — Emerging Africa Infrastructure Fund (EAIF) / GuarantCo
title: Raxio Data Centres - EAIF Debt Financing
description: EAIF provided USD 33 million in debt financing to Raxio Data Centres, a pan-African data centre platform. Raxio operates carrier-neutral data centres in Uganda, Ethiopia, DRC, Cote d'Ivoire, Mozambique, Angola and Tanzania. The investment reduces latency and improves digital infrastructure across sub-Saharan Africa, supporting cloud adoption and digital transformation.
notes: IATI commit: USD 33,188,000 type-2 transaction 2023-12-31. Activity start 2023-04-13. No recipient country in IATI -> REG (countries: UGA, ETH, COD, CIV, MOZ, AGO, TZA per EAIF project page). Primary cat: 250200 Data Infrastructure (data centres).

title: PARTECH AFRICA VENTURE CAPITAL FUND I
description: EIB equity participation in Partech Africa Fund I, first Africa-focused VC fund by Partech. EUR 124M fund launched 2018. EIB committed EUR 10M. Targets digital and technology start-ups across Africa. Successfully deployed.
co_financiers: Partech and other LPs
notes: EIB portal confirms EUR 10M commitment, vintage year 2017, fund size EUR 124M.

title: RURAL TELECOM NETWORK EXPANSION (DRC AND CAMEROON)
description: Construction and installation of mobile telecom networks to extend coverage to rural communities of Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) and Cameroon currently not served. Networks using frequency bands under revenue share agreements. By end 2018: coverage to over 3.6M people via 650 sites. Extends mobile population coverage from 75% to 80% in DRC and 85% to 95% in Cameroon.
notes: Multi-country: DRC and Cameroon. EUR 26M. Signed Dec 2016. Rural coverage expansion.

title: EAST AND CENTRAL AFRICA OPTICAL FIBER ROLL OUT
description: Financing for optical fibre roll-out across East and Central Africa. Supports high-speed broadband deployment in underserved areas. EUR 54M EIB commitment.
notes: IATI region 298 (Africa regional). EUR 54M. Signed Nov 2018. Completed Dec 2020.

title: PARTECH AFRICA VENTURE CAPITAL FUND II
description: Equity participation in Partech Africa II, a EUR 230M venture capital fund focusing on start-ups and high-growth SMEs active in digital and technology sectors in Africa. Fund targets local start-ups in digital/tech sectors across Africa. EIB committed EUR 20M as anchor investor. Co-investors include KfW, IFC, FMO, BII, DEG, Bpifrance. Successor to Partech Africa Fund I.
co_financiers: KfW; IFC; FMO; BII; DEG; Bpifrance
notes: EIB committed EUR 20M (estimate per task brief; fund size EUR 230M). Afrikan Heroes report confirms EIB anchor investor alongside KfW, IFC, FMO, BII, DEG, Bpifrance. SSA-focused fund.

title: AXIAN TELECOM EXPANSION (MADAGASCAR AND TANZANIA)
description: Financing package to AXIAN Telecom to expand 4G mobile broadband network infrastructure across Madagascar and Tanzania, and introduction of 5G coverage. Global Gateway investment backed by EU budgetary guarantee. USD 60M for Tanzania, USD 40M for Madagascar. Supports inclusive digitalisation and sustainable development.
notes: USD 100M: USD 60M Tanzania, USD 40M Madagascar. Signed Dec 2024 per IATI. EIB press release April 2025. IATI shows EUR 95.1M equivalent.

recip_org: AFRISTAT; INSEE; FERDI; national tax and customs administrations
title: DATAFID — Regional Project to Support Strengthening of Digital Economy Statistics and Use of Data Science by Tax and Customs Administrations
description: DATAFID aims to increase knowledge about the digital economy and its impacts, and to build the capacities of tax and customs administrations through improved use of data science. Two components: (1) improving knowledge of the digital economy to include it in the tax system; (2) improving the transparency and performance of tax and customs administrations. Beneficiary countries: Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, Niger, Senegal, Togo (Component 1); Côte d'Ivoire, Mauritania, Niger, Togo (Component 2). Implemented with AFRISTAT, INSEE, FERDI. Financed by the French Directorate-General of the Treasury (DG Trésor) under the Strategic Investment Plan for Development (PISD) 2020–2023.
notes: Budget: €1.5M per EF project page; €2.5M per PISD 2020-2023 annual report (incl. FERDI sub-grant allocated by EF). commit-orig set to 2500000 per PISD funder-of-record document; amount-quality=Estimated. EUR→USD at 2021 rate ~1.18. Countries: CMR, CIV, NER, SEN, TGO (Component 1); CIV, MRT, NER, TGO (Component 2). All SSA-eligible. MRT included (not North Africa). Funder: French Treasury (DG Trésor) PISD 2020-2023, not EU pass-through.

title: Partech Africa Fund II SSA Tech Startup VC Fund
description: FMO equity commitment of EUR 25M to Partech Africa Fund II, a generalist tech venture capital fund targeting early-stage fintech, mobile consumer services, mobility, supply chain, and digital economy startups in Sub-Saharan Africa. Fund closed at EUR 263M/USD 300M+ with KfW as anchor.
co_financiers: KfW; EIB; IFC; BII; DEG; Proparco; Bpifrance; Commercial LPs
notes: FMO NV equity EUR25M effective 2022-12-22. Partech Africa II closed at USD300M+. Not in FMO IATI SSA dataset (booked Africa-wide). SSA-focused fund: fintech, mobile, digital economy.

title: TIDE Africa II SSA Early-Stage Tech VC Fund (TLcom Capital)
description: FMO Ventures Program USD 10M commitment to TIDE Africa II LP, TLcom Capital's second Africa-focused VC fund. Invests in early-stage tech and tech-enabled companies at Seed and Series A in East and West Africa. Fund closed at USD 154M.
co_financiers: EIB; AfricaGrow; Visa Foundation; Bertelsmann; Others
notes: FMO Ventures Program USD10M effective 2024-04-19. TIDE Africa II final close April 2024 at USD154M. TLcom offices in Lagos and Nairobi; sector-agnostic digital focus.

recip_org: Microsoft Africa Development Centre (ADC) — Nairobi and Lagos
title: Microsoft Africa Development Centre (ADC) — $100M Engineering Hub Nairobi and Lagos
description: Microsoft launched the Africa Development Centre (ADC) in May 2019 with initial hubs in Nairobi, Kenya and Lagos, Nigeria — Microsoft's 7th global development centre and first in Africa. Committed $100 million over the first 5 years to recruit 500 full-time engineers building cloud, AI/ML, mixed reality, and Windows products with global impact. By 2022, ADC employed ~570 engineers. Lagos facility expanded March 2022 in Ikoyi. Note: Nigeria ADC closed in 2024 per Bloomberg.
notes: $100M explicitly committed for ADC over 5 years (confirmed Forbes, TechPoint, NIPC, Microsoft statements). KEN + NGA dual-location -> REG. Period 2019-2023/2024. Nigeria ADC closed 2024 (Bloomberg report May 2024). Nairobi facility expanded with $27M facility opened March 2022.

title: Digital Frontiers Institute (DFI) — Phases I and II
description: FCDO grant through FSD Africa to the Digital Frontiers Institute for capacity building in digital finance. Supports professionals in Sub-Saharan Africa and globally through digital finance certification programmes including the Certificate in Digital Money (CIDM) and Certified Digital Finance Practitioner (CDFP). Phase I and Phase II ran from 2016 to 2022. Builds regulatory and practitioner capacity for digital financial services ecosystems across SSA priority countries. Part of FCDO's support to financial sector deepening in Africa through FSD Africa.
notes: Source: FSD Africa project document 2022. Total FCDO £2,714,012 for DFI Phases I+II. SSA attribution 70%. FX 2016 = 1.3551. amount-quality=Imputed. v3 sweep: cat-primary recoded 250600→251200 (Digital Skills And Jobs) — digital finance certification/practitioner training; flag-digital-payments=TRUE (digital finance, digital money, digital financial services explicitly named); 250300 removed from cat-add-codes (no AI/emerging tech basis).

title: Women in the Digital Economy Fund (WiDEF) - GSMA
description: USAID GenDev buy-in for Women in the Digital Economy Fund, implemented by GSMA. Supports mobile internet access and digital financial services for women across Africa.
notes: IATI commit $11M. Region 998. GSMA Connected Women program with Africa focus.

recip_org: TLcom Capital / TIDE Africa Fund II
title: TIDE Africa Fund II — Venture Capital for Digital Start-ups Sub-Saharan Africa (Fund II)
description: Equity LP commitment to TLcom Capital's TIDE Africa Fund II, a USD 154 million venture capital fund dedicated to technology-enabled start-ups in Sub-Saharan Africa. Fund II targets early-stage companies leveraging digital technologies across fintech, agritech, edtech, healthtech. Proparco participated as LP investor alongside BII, IFC, BIO, DEG, Allianz.
co_financiers: IFC; British International Investment (BII); BIO; DEG; Allianz; CDC Group
notes: TLcom TIDE Africa Fund II final close at USD 154M (2024). Proparco confirmed as LP by multiple sources (Swedfund, BIO, How We Made It in Africa). Exact Proparco ticket not disclosed. title: Moov Africa SSA Subsidiaries Annual CapEx 2024 - Network and Licence Investment
description: Maroc Telecom's capital expenditures in its sub-Saharan African subsidiaries operating under the Moov Africa brand reached MAD 7.96 billion ($800 million) in 2024, up 75.6% from MAD 4.54 billion in 2023. Of the total, MAD 3.549 billion was for frequency and licence acquisitions. title: China Eximbank provides RMB 530 million government concessional loan for Phase 3 of the E-Government Project (Linked to Project ID#58382 and #58409)
description: On September 10, 2007, China Eximbank and the Government of Senegal signed an RMB 390 million government concessional loan (GCL) agreement for Phase 1 of the E-Government Project. Phase 1 established data, voice, and video conference systems for government agencies, universities, hospitals, and other state-owned enterprises and institutions by laying 500 km of fiber optic cable — in Dakar, Thiès, Louga, Saint Louis, Diourbel, Kaolack and Fatick — and created data centers to support the e-government network (also known as the government intranet). It also involved the creation of a fixed, IP and mobile telephony network covering 11 regional capitals, including Dakar, Thiès, Louga, Saint Louis, Diourbel, Kaolack, Fatick, Matam, Tambacounda. Huawei (中国华为公司) and China Machinery Engineering Corporation (中国机械设备进出口公司) were responsible for implementation.   Construction began on December 12, 2007, and Phase 1 was officially completed and handed over to the authorities on July 10, 2009. Then, in January 2012, China Eximbank and the Government of Senegal signed a $50 million loan agreement for Phase 2 of the E-Government Project. This loan reportedly carried the following terms: a 20-year maturity and 2.75% interest rate.  Phase 2, which began in December 2012 and ended in late 2014, involved an expansion of the country’s national fiber optic backbone and administrative intranet. More specifically, it involved the laying of 1,000 additional kilometers of fiber optic cable in Mbour, Bignona, Sédhiou, Tambacounda, Koungueul, Kaffrine, Kaolack, Fatick, Tamba, Kolda, Vélingara, Ziguinchor, Saint-Louis, Louga, Diourbel, and Blaise Diagne International Airport (AIBD). Huawei was responsible for the implementation of Phase 2. Then, on November 24, 2015, China Eximbank and the Government of Senegal signed an RMB 530 million government concessional loan (GCL) agreement for Phase 3 of the E-Government Project.   The terms of this loan are unknown, As of February 19, 2019, total disburs

title: China Eximbank provides RMB 1.05 billion government concessional loan for Phase 1 of Smart Senegal Project (Linked to Project ID#92565)
description: On February 8, 2018, during a meeting between the Chinese Ambassador to Senegal Zhang Xun and Senegalese Minister of Economy, Finance and Planning Amadou Ba, the Government of Senegal and China Eximbank signed an RMB 1.05 billion government concessional loan (GCL) agreement and an RMB 200 million grant agreement for Phase 1 of the Smart Senegal Project. The GCL is captured via February 8, 2018. The RMB 200 million grant is captured via Project ID#92565. According to the Government of Senegal’s Aid Management Platform, the GCL disbursed in 2019 and 2020 and achieved an overall disbursement rate of 100%.   This project aims to leverage information and communication technology to reduce the digital deficit and make Senegal a sub-regional hub in West Africa, particularly in the areas of security, education, health, and tourism. The Smart Senegal Project consists of five major sub-projects: (1) Safe City: This sub-project aims to strengthen public security through the use of technological tools and processes for the collection, analysis of flows, events from various communication systems and dissemination of information; (2) Smart Education: This sub-project project aims on the one hand to modernize network, processing, and data storage infrastructures and on the other hand to deploy technological platforms to improve teaching and learning in universities, schools elementary, middle and high schools in Senegal. (3) Smart Territories: This sub-project aims to provide user-centered services (citizens, administrations, and businesses) by building a citizen’s house (“La Maison du Citoyen”) in all the country's departments; (4) Submarine Cable: The purpose of this sub-project is to land a submarine cable in Senegal allowing access to international bandwidth. This new device will increase Senegal's internet capacity and vary the Internet access offer for ISPs; and (5) Smart Wifi: The Smart Wifi sub-project project aims to fight against the digital isolation of territories by i

title: Chinese Government provides RMB 200 million grant in 2018 for Phase 1 of Smart Senegal Project (Linked to Project ID#73114)
description: On February 8, 2018, during a meeting between the Chinese Ambassador to Senegal Zhang Xun and Senegalese Minister of Economy, Finance and Planning Amadou Ba, the Government of Senegal and China Eximbank signed an RMB 1.05 billion government concessional loan (GCL) agreement and an RMB 200 million grant agreement for Phase 1 of the Smart Senegal Project. The GCL is captured via February 8, 2018. The RMB 200 million grant is captured via Project ID#92565. According to the Government of Senegal’s Aid Management Platform, the GCL disbursed in 2019 and 2020 and achieved an overall disbursement rate of 100%.   This project aims to leverage information and communication technology to reduce the digital deficit and make Senegal a sub-regional hub in West Africa, particularly in the areas of security, education, health, and tourism. The Smart Senegal Project consists of five major sub-projects: (1) Safe City: This sub-project aims to strengthen public security through the use of technological tools and processes for the collection, analysis of flows, events from various communication systems and dissemination of information; (2) Smart Education: This sub-project project aims on the one hand to modernize network, processing, and data storage infrastructures and on the other hand to deploy technological platforms to improve teaching and learning in universities, schools elementary, middle and high schools in Senegal. (3) Smart Territories: This sub-project aims to provide user-centered services (citizens, administrations, and businesses) by building a citizen’s house (“La Maison du Citoyen”) in all the country's departments; (4) Submarine Cable: The purpose of this sub-project is to land a submarine cable in Senegal allowing access to international bandwidth. This new device will increase Senegal's internet capacity and vary the Internet access offer for ISPs; and (5) Smart Wifi: The Smart Wifi sub-project project aims to fight against the digital isolation of territories by i

title: Axian Consortium Acquisition of Tigo Senegal (now Yas/Free Sénégal)
description: On 2 May 2018, a consortium of Axian Group (via Sofima), NJJ Capital (Xavier Niel) and Teyliom (Yerim Habib Sow) — operating as Saga Africa Holdings — acquired 100% of Sentel/Tigo Sénégal from Millicom International. Tigo was Senegal's second-largest mobile operator. The consortium committed an investment programme of approximately 70 billion CFA francs (~USD 107 million at 2018 FX) for network expansion, 4G roll-out, and mobile banking (Free Money). The operator was rebranded as Free Sénégal in October 2019, and later rebranded again to Yas Sénégal. In July 2023 Axian increased its stake from 40% to 80% (no financial figure disclosed for this subsequent transaction).

recip_org: Sierra Leone Ministry of Finance and Economic Development
title: China Eximbank provides RMB 207 million government concessional loan for Phase 2 of National Fibre Optic Backbone Infrastructure Project (Linked to Project ID#30254)
description: On June 23, 2019, the Chinese Government and the Government of Sierra Leone signed a preferential loan framework agreement for Phase 2 of the National Fibre Optic Backbone Infrastructure Project. Then, on August 26, 2019, China Eximbank and the the Government of Sierra Leone’s Ministry of Finance signed an RMB 207,000,000 government concessional loan (GCL) agreement [CHINA EXIMBANK GCL NO (2019) 10 TOTAL No. (681)] for Phase 2 of the National Fibre Optic Backbone Infrastructure Project. The GCL agreement was then ratified by Sierra Leone’s Parliament on September 19, 2019. The borrowing terms of the GCL are as follows: a 20-year maturity, a 5-year grace period, a 1% interest rate, a 0.25% (RMB 517,500) management fee, and a 0.25% commitment fee. The borrower is responsible for repaying the loan between 2024 and 2039. Under the terms of the agreement, Government of Sierra Leone’s Ministry of Finance is also responsible for on-lending the proceeds of the GCL to a state-owned enterprise in Sierra Leone known as Sierra Leone Cable Company Limited (SALCAB). SALCAB, in turn, is expected to use the proceeds of the GCL to finance a $30 million commercial contract (ID#0002881900010V) between the Government of Sierra Leone’s Ministry of Information and Communications and Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd., which was signed on April 11, 2019. SALCAB signed an on-lending agreement with the Ministry of Finance on August 22, 2019.   The purpose of the project is to lay a 721.5-kilometer fiber-optic network that covers 15 major cities in the northern, eastern and southern regions of Sierra Leone.  Upon completion, the telecommunications infrastructure is expected to allow telecommunications operators to offer 3G and 4G services.   Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd. is the EPC contractor responsible for implementation. On April 24, 2020, the Government of Sierra Leone received the relevant technical equipment for project implementation. By September 2020, construction had commenced. Then, on

recip_org: Electricity Generation and Transmission Company (EGTC), Government of Sierra Leone / MCA-Sierra Leone
title: Sierra Leone Compact — Transmission Dispatch Center and SCADA Upgrades (Transmission Backbone Project, Activity 1.1)
description: Activity 1.1 of MCC's $480.7M Sierra Leone Compact (signed September 2024) establishes a national transmission dispatch center with supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA) system for EGTC. The dispatch center provides centralised visibility and remote operating capability over the transmission network, enabling real-time monitoring and control of the 225kV transmission backbone. SCADA upgrades improve grid reliability, accelerate response to network disruptions, facilitate integration of variable renewable generation sources, and support future regional integration with neighbouring countries. The dispatch center also includes a backup facility. The $23.4M activity is part of the $226.7M Transmission Backbone Project. No digital identity or payments components; purely energy sector operational technology / SCADA digitisation.
notes: Activity 1.1 of the Transmission Backbone Project. Compact signed 2024-09-27. Not yet entered into force as of June 2025 per MCC project page (still Signed status). Total Transmission Backbone Project = $226,702,000; this row attributes only the dispatch center / SCADA activity. Co-attribution with Distribution Dispatch Center row (deal-id: mcc-sle-scada-distribution-2024). title: Chinese Government provides $6 million grant for Phase 1 of Educational Technical Cooperation Project (Linked to Project ID#92527)
description: In March 2015, the Chinese Government and the Government of South Sudan agreed to conduct a feasibility study for the Educational Technical Cooperation Project. The feasibility study was completed in May 2015. Then, on February 3, 2016, the Chinese Government and the Government of South Sudan signed an exchange of letters regarding Phase 1 of the Educational Technical Cooperation Project. China’s Ministry of Commerce (MOFCOM) reportedly committed $6 million of grant funding for the project’s first phase (captured via Project ID#59201).  Phase 1 involved the design of courses, the training for over 200 South Sudanese teachers in China, the printing of over 1,299,000 textbooks for use by 150,000 teachers and students, and the construction of training centers. China South Publishing and Media Group (中南出版传媒集团股份有限公司) was the contractor responsible for project implementation. Phase 1 officially commenced on June 29, 2016. One month later, on July 29, 2016, MOFCOM awarded a contract to China South Publishing and Media Group. Then, on December 23, 2016, a project implementation agreement was signed by the Chinese Government and the Government of South Sudan. The first phase of teacher training activities was completed in April 2017. During Phase 1, 54 teachers were trained including 14 members of parliament who were members of the education committee. Then, on July 14, 2017, the first batch of 745,000 textbooks and the equipment of the ICT teacher training center arrived in Juba. The second batch of 554,000 textbooks was scheduled for printing by the end of 2017. The project’s first phase passed a mid-term inspection by MOFCOM in December 2017. A one-month ICT training was successfully completed in South Sudan in February 2018. The project’s ICT infrastructure and ICT training component was completed in September 2018.
