title: China Eximbank provides $248.8 million loan for Air Traffic Management System (ATMS) Project
description: On January 25, 2018, the Chinese Ambassador to South Sudan and the South Sudanese Finance Minister signed a preferential loan framework agreement. On the same day, the Government of South Sudan and China Eximbank signed a $248.8 million loan agreement for the Air Traffic Management System (ATMS) Project. The borrowing terms of the loan are unknown. However, it is known the borrower was expected to use the proceeds of the loan to finance a commercial (EPC) contract between South Sudan Civil Aviation Authority and China Harbor Engineering Company (CHEC) Limited, which was signed on October 14, 2016. This loan is reportedly secured by (collateralized against) airport fees/revenues that the borrower is required to deposit in a lender-controlled escrow account.  The loan's amount outstanding was $231,952,567 as of December 2022.  The purpose of the project is to construct Juba ATMS center, a new Air Traffic control tower and operation building, a surveillance system, a navigation system, and a communication system. The Juba ATMS center is located next to Juba International Airport. The project also involves the civil engineering and supporting facilities required by the equipment (such as meteorological facilities), calibration flight test training, and other forms of training for South Sudanese technicians (to prepare them for management of the air space by 2023).   CHEC is the EPC contractor responsible for implementation. Project implementation commenced in June 2020 and it was originally expected to reach completion by June 2023. On July 6, 2021, South Sudan Civil Aviation Authority (SSCAA) announced the launch of a state-of-the-art online system, which allows the opening of new routes. The newly established lower airspace, launched in partnership with aviation solutions provider NavPass, is designed to boost tourism and generate revenue for the economy, according to SSCAA. Through the system, operators of commercial, cargo, and aid aircraft can integrate Juba Intern

recip_org: CWS Investment Ltd / Cable & Wireless Seychelles
title: Cable & Wireless Seychelles Acquisition Financing
description: TDB served as Mandated Lead Arranger for a USD 70 million syndicated acquisition financing for CWS Investment Ltd (a group of Seychellois entrepreneurs) to acquire Cable & Wireless Seychelles (CWS) from Liberty Latin America. TDB provided USD 50 million of its own balance sheet, with co-lending from Absa Group Limited and ABC Banking Corporation of Mauritius. CWS is Seychelles' leading ICT/telecoms operator, providing fixed-line, mobile, broadband, and FTTH services, placing CWS into 100% local ownership for the first time. The deal was TDB's largest transaction in Seychelles to date.
co_financiers: Absa Group Limited; ABC Banking Corporation of Mauritius; Nouvobanq Seychelles
notes: TDB own ticket = USD 50M (confirmed in 2020 Sustainability Report: 'TDB provided USD 50 million of the syndicated loan'). Total deal = USD 107.5M; total debt arranged by TDB = USD 70M. CWS pre-listing statement confirms USD 70M loan facility in November 2019. By June 2022, outstanding was USD 50M, then restructured. SYC is SSA-49. No Egypt component. ICT/telecoms acquisition loan.

title: Cable & Wireless Seychelles Debt Refinancing and Digital Transformation Facility
description: TDB provided a USD 50 million funding package to refinance Cable & Wireless Seychelles' outstanding debt of USD 48.1 million from the original 2019 acquisition loan. The package includes bridging and term loan facilities, a USD 5 million convertible note (convertible to CWS equity at time of share listing), and a term loan in Seychelles Rupees. The transaction also supports CWS's digital transformation activities including eBill and self-care platforms, online registrations, and bill payments. CWS is exploring fintech innovations through Nebula Fintech Limited. Following the restructuring, TDB also acquired approximately 2.9% equity stake in CWS via an off-market transaction (December 2022). CWS subsequently listed on MERJ Exchange Seychelles in September 2022.
co_financiers: Nouvobanq Seychelles (co-arranger on subsequent bond issuance)
notes: 2023 Sustainability Report: 'USD 50 million funding package... aimed at refinancing USD 48.1 million in debt.' Pre-listing statement: debt restructured in June 2022 into (i) new USD 45M facility with better terms, (ii) USD 5M convertible note. TDB equity acquisition disclosed 22/12/2022 (2.9% stake). TDB also co-arranged CWS's first local-currency corporate bond in Seychelles Rupees in February 2024 (SCR 168M ~USD 12.5M). This row covers the 2022 debt refinancing facility. title: Chinese Government provides RMB 170 million grant for ICT Modernization Project (Linked to Project ID#92012)
description: On June 26, 2019, the Chinese Ambassador to Chad, Li Jinjin, and Chad’s Minister of Economy and Development Planning, Issa Dubrani, signed a preferential loan framework agreement for the ICT Modernization Project. On the same day, China Eximbank and the Government of Chad signed an RMB 1.316 billion (CFA 111.4 billion or $189 million) government concessional loan (GCL) agreement for the ICT Modernization Project (as captured via Project ID#92012). The loan agreement was subsequently approved unanimously by Chad’s Parliament on April 30, 2020. The loan carries the following borrowing terms: a 20 year maturity, a 5 year grace period, and a 2% interest rate. It is secured by (i.e. collateralized against) a minimum cash balance in an escrow account.  The borrower is expected to use the proceeds of the grant and loan to finance a commercial (EPC) contract with Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd.   The Chinese Government also issued an RMB 170 million grant in 2019 for the ICT Modernization Project (as captured via Project ID#91949).  The project has three components: the construction of a national data center; the construction of a 1200 km optic fiber cable network spanning the country from south to east (along the Doba-Koumra-Sarh-Kyabé-Am Timan-Abéché-Am Zoer-Guéréda-Iriba axis); and the construction of 50 km loop of fiber optic cables in the vicinity of N’Djamena with 200 2G/3G/4G sites.   Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd. is the EPC contractor responsible for implementation. A groundbreaking ceremony took place on July 7, 2020. According to the Government of Chad's Aid Management Platform, the project's originally expected commencement date was May 1, 2020 and its originally expected completion date was October 31, 2021.

The financing will fund construction of ~200 new telecom sites across Tanzania, increasing coverage in rural areas and bridging the digital divide. Helios Towers is Africa's leading independent telecoms infrastructure company, operating towers on over 14,000 sites and serving 144+ million mobile subscribers across Africa. The investment directly enables mobile internet and data services for underserved rural populations.

recip_org: MIC Tanzania Ltd (Tigo Tanzania) / Zanzibar Telecom (Zantel)
title: Axian-Rostam Consortium Acquisition of Tigo Tanzania and Zantel from Millicom
description: On 7 April 2022, a consortium led by Axian Telecom (together with Rostam Azizi) completed the acquisition of Millicom's entire Tanzanian telecom operations: MIC Tanzania Ltd (Tigo Tanzania) and Zanzibar Telecom (Zantel). The deal price was USD 100 million. The acquisition gave Axian strategic ownership in the EASSy cable landing station in Dar es Salaam, a consortium network of over 3,200 km backbone and 600 km metro fibre. The business was subsequently rebranded as Yas Tanzania. Axian announced a USD 500 million investment plan for Tanzanian network expansion over subsequent years.
co_financiers: Rostam Azizi (co-acquirer, share undisclosed)
notes: USD 100M sale price confirmed by Connecting Africa (citing Millicom disclosures). Millicom Q1 2022 earnings confirmed the Tanzania exit. Axian's USD 500M Tanzania investment plan announced April 2021 (Bloomberg) — separate multi-year capex commitment not recorded separately as amounts are future commitments not contractually bound in a single instrument.

title: Vodacom Tanzania M-Pesa Platform Modernisation — Fintech 2.0
description: Vodacom Tanzania PLC completed a $28 million investment to migrate its M-Pesa platform from the legacy G2 system to a next-generation 'Fintech 2.0' platform, announced April 2026. The 15-month project, completed April 2026, modernises Tanzania's leading mobile money platform (30 million customers, ~41% market share, 600,000+ merchants). Key improvements: 1,000+ transactions/second capacity; system updates without service interruption (previously required nightly downtime); enhanced security; faster product deployment. The upgrade is part of a wider $100M modernisation programme announced Sep 2025 (Vodacom Tanzania 25th anniversary). The new platform also supports Tanzania becoming the first African country to enable M-Pesa contactless (tap-to-pay) payments.
notes: $28M investment for M-Pesa Fintech 2.0 migration (G2 → new platform). 15-month project. Part of wider $100M Vodacom Tanzania modernisation (Sep 2025 announcement). Tanzania first African market to launch M-Pesa tap-to-pay (contactless). flag-digital-payments=TRUE: core mobile money infrastructure. Source: Vodacom Group press release 2 Apr 2026; TheFastMode Apr 2026. Commit-year=2026 (announcement/completion date).

title: ZTE grants $3.5 million USD for the Safe Zanzibar Project
description: In June 2015, ZTE corporation donated $3.5 million USD for the Safe Zanzibar Project which involves installing a CCTV surveillance system in Stone Town. The purpose of this project was to curb crime in Zanzibar, particularly in tourist areas.   According to the reporting from the Habarileo, the entire project was scheduled for completion by the end of 2015. Work plans included the installation of 300 full high definition cameras (FHD) on the main roads of the Stone Town and a command center in Malindi with a video wall and camera control center. The video surveillance system uses Geographic Information System (GIS), case library, and intelligent analysis tools. According to the engineer, with routine management, this system can facilitate sharing information among different administrative departments, utilizing security resources and improving efficiency  of controlling or responding rapidly to major accidents.
notes: Safe City (Zanzibar): urban surveillance/security system by ZTE. Includes CCTV and monitoring components. | AidData staff note: nan

title: Chinese Government provides RMB 200 million grant for Customs Modernization Project in Uganda
description: On June 28, 2017, the Chinese Ministry of Commerce signed a grant agreement with the Government of Uganda worth RMB 200 million for the Customs Modernization Project. The grant agreement was signed in the Chinese capital by Matia Kasaija, Minister of Finance, Planning, and Economic Development and Dr. Qian Keming, Vice Minister of Commerce of the People's Republic of China. The grant will support the capacity of Uganda Revenue Authority (URA) to acquire latest technologies in the form of non-intrusive scanners, modernized customs risk management, supervision and communication systems, customs service and enforcement support, and border infrastructure among others. All of these advancements are intended to allow Uganda to increase the revenue collected by their Customs Agency.

title: China Eximbank provides RMB 96.9 million government concessional loan for Phase 3 of National Transmission Backbone and e-Government Infrastructure Project (Linked to Project ID#53401 and Project ID#53402)
description: On March 31, 2015, the Chinese Government and the Government of Uganda signed a preferential loan framework agreement for Phase 3 of the National Transmission Backbone and e-Government Infrastructure Project. On the same day, China Eximbank and the Government of Uganda (represented by the Ministry of Finance, Planning, and Economic Development) signed an RMB 96,966,600 ($15,391,522) government concessional loan (GCL) agreement [CHINA EXIMBANK GCL NO. (2015) 4 TOTAL NO. (543)] for Phase 3 of the National Transmission Backbone and e-Government Infrastructure Project. The Government of Uganda’s Ministry of Finance, Planning, and Economic Development then on-lent the proceeds of the GCL to National Information Technology Authority Uganda (NITA-U). The borrowing terms of the GCL were as follows: a 20 year maturity, a 5 year grace period, a 2% interest rate, a 0% default (penalty) interest rate, a 0.25% commitment fee, and a 0.25% management fee. The GCL is secured by (collateralized against) a minimum cash balance in a lender-controlled bank account (see staff comment #6 for more details). The proceeds of the GCL were to be used by the borrower to finance a $15,391,522 commercial (turnkey) contract [ID#0008000610060C] between the Government of Uganda’s Ministry of Information and Communication Technology and Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd., which was signed on July 15, 2010 and amended on December 30, 2013.   According to the Government of Uganda’s Aid Management Platform (AMP), this loan achieved a 94.8% disbursement rate, with China Eximbank making x loan disbursements (worth $14,604,878.74) between 2015 and 2017: a $4,619,644.65 disbursement on September 30, 2015, a $5,192,303.56 disbursement on September 30, 2016, a $2,145,289.98 disbursement on April 19, 2017, a $2,553,844.51 disbursement on June 28, 2017, and a $93,796.05 disbursement on December 21, 2017. According to the Uganda Ministry of Finance, Planning, and Economic Development's Report on Public Debt, Grants,
notes: AidData staff note: 1. The China Eximbank loan (GCL) agreement can be accessed in its entirety via https://www.dropbox.c

title: Liquid Telecom South Africa Term Loan
description: IFC provided a $100 million senior term loan to Liquid Telecommunications South Africa (Pty) Ltd, the South African subsidiary of Liquid Intelligent Technologies (formerly Liquid Telecom). The financing supports expansion of fibre optic network capacity and data centre operations across South Africa, enabling affordable broadband for businesses and consumers. South Africa is Liquid's largest single market and home to its Johannesburg-based data centres serving as regional hubs for cloud, colocation, and IP transit services.
notes: IATI: XM-DAC-903-SII-44830; status=2 (Active); actual start 2021-06-28; finance type 411 (senior loan). Transaction value $100M. title: Equinix Johannesburg JN1 Data Centre — US$160 Million Investment
description: Equinix announced in December 2022 its entry into South Africa with a US$160 million data centre investment in Johannesburg, named JN1. The facility, expected to open mid-2024, represents Equinix's second African country entry following the MainOne acquisition. JN1 provides carrier-neutral colocation and interconnection services in Johannesburg, a major digital hub for southern Africa and gateway to hyperscaler cloud on-ramps. TechCabal (June 2025) reports Equinix has opened its first data centre in Johannesburg.
notes: Equinix investor relations press release Dec 13 2022. $160M greenfield in Johannesburg. JN1 open mid-2024 per announcement; TechCabal Jun 2025 confirms Johannesburg DC operational. Separate from MainOne/West Africa acquisition. No DFI co-financing disclosed.

title: AfricaGrow LP commitment to Knife Capital Fund III
description: AfricaGrow commits to Knife Capital's $50M Series B expansion fund (Knife Fund III) targeting high-growth African technology companies at Series A/B stage. Focus on ICT, fintech, e-commerce, SaaS, healthtech. South Africa-based with pan-African portfolio.
co_financiers: IFC, SA SME Fund, Standard Bank, Skybound Capital, Fireball Capital
notes: AfricaGrow confirmed LP in Knife Capital Fund III per SAVCA press release. Fund closed $50M Aug 2023. Primary base South Africa but SSA portfolio. Ticket size not disclosed; estimated $5M per AfricaGrow strategy range (EUR 5-15M VC fund investments).

recip_org: Independent Communications Authority of South Africa (ICASA) / MTN South Africa
title: MTN South Africa 5G/4G Spectrum Acquisition — 2022 ICASA Auction
description: MTN South Africa acquired 100 MHz of high-demand radio frequency spectrum across three bands in the ICASA multi-band spectrum auction concluded 17 March 2022: 2x10MHz in the 800MHz band (R2.9bn / $195.5M), 4x10MHz in the 2600MHz band (R1.1bn / $72.8M), and 4x10MHz in the 3500MHz band (R1.1bn / $75.1M). Total cost: R5.2 billion. This spectrum enables 4G capacity expansion and 5G network rollout across South Africa. R3.3bn was paid to ICASA in April 2022, with the remaining R1.9bn paid in H2 2023 once the 800MHz band was cleared of interference. The auction was South Africa's first spectrum auction in over a decade.
notes: ICASA auction 10-17 March 2022 (58 rounds). MTN SA secured 100MHz: 800MHz (2x10MHz, R2.9bn/$195.5M), 2600MHz (4x10MHz, R1.1bn/$72.8M), 3500MHz (4x10MHz, R1.1bn/$75.1M). Total R5.2bn. commit-usd = sum of SENS-stated USD band equivalents: $195.5M+$72.8M+$75.1M=$343.4M. R3.3bn paid Apr 2022; R1.9bn paid H2 2023. Spectrum licence period 20 years (end-year=2042). Source: MTN SENS JSE 17 Mar 2022. Tower divestiture proceeds (~R5.2bn IHS Towers SA) used to fund this — the tower SALE is NOT recorded here (MTN divestiture, not investment).

title: Norrsken22 co-leads TymeBank pre-Series C — South African digital bank
description: Norrsken22 co-leads (with Blue Earth Capital) a $77.8M pre-Series C round for TymeBank, South Africa's leading digital-only challenger bank serving the mass market. TymeBank serves over 9 million customers with zero-fee banking and micro-credit. The round will further expand operations in South Africa and the Philippines. Tencent increases its stake in the round alongside new lead investors Norrsken22 and Blue Earth Capital.
co_financiers: Blue Earth Capital; Tencent; African Rainbow Capital; JG Summit Holdings
notes: Round total $77.8M co-led by Norrsken22 and Blue Earth Capital. Norrsken22 ticket estimated ~$15M based on fund average ticket size of $10-16M; two co-leads suggest ~equal split. TymeBank is SSA-registered and operates primarily in South Africa. flag-digital-payments=TRUE as core product is digital bank with payments. Series noted as pre-Series C in press release, labeled Series B in some sources.

title: Actis-led consortium acquisition of Swiftnet telecom tower platform from Telkom South Africa
description: Actis, together with Royal Bafokeng Holdings as empowerment partner, acquires 100% of Swiftnet, Telkom South Africa's masts and towers portfolio, at an enterprise value of ZAR 6.75 billion (c. USD 355 million). Actis leads the consortium via Towerco Bidco with a 70% stake; Royal Bafokeng Holdings holds 30%. Swiftnet operates approximately 4,000 towers and masts across South Africa. Transaction completed March 2025 following regulatory approvals from Competition Tribunal (September 2024) and ICASA (December 2024).
co_financiers: Royal Bafokeng Holdings
notes: EV USD 355M at announcement (ZAR 6.75bn). Actis 70% stake in Towerco Bidco = equity attributable ~USD 248M, but total EV used as commit-usd per convention. Agreed March 2024, completed March 2025. Royal Bafokeng Holdings holds 30% as SA empowerment partner. Standard Bank acted as financial arranger. ~4,000 towers. ZAR/USD rate: 2024 average ~18.9 applied for USD 355M confirmation.

title: Actis acquisition of 85% stake in Octotel fibre network operator South Africa
description: Actis acquires an 85% stake in Octotel, a Cape Town-based fibre-to-the-home (FTTH) network operator focused on the Western Cape, South Africa. Investment of approximately USD 167 million from the Neoma Africa Fund III. Octotel's founders remain as minority shareholders. Strategy focused on expanding homes passed with fibre and driving take-up rates. Actis led a ZAR 2bn social loan facility from RMB in 2022. Actis exited to consortium led by AIIM, STOA and Thebe Investment Corporation in September 2024.
notes: Fund: Neoma Africa Fund III (fund size confidential). Stake: 85%. Exit date: September 2024 to AIIM-led consortium (AIIM + STOA + Thebe Investment Corporation). GPCA deal book confirms USD 167M and 85% stake. Enterprise value at acquisition was ZAR 2.3bn (~USD 140M per Oct 2020 reports; USD 167M per GPCA). Used GPCA/Actis official figure of USD 167M as authoritative.

financier_name: British International Investment (BII, formerly CDC Group)
title: Too Much Wifi — Debt Investment (Wireless Broadband ISP)
description: BII committed $4.4M to Too Much Wifi (TMWF) in December 2024, a wireless broadband connectivity provider offering uncapped, low-cost, high-speed internet services in South Africa, targeting underserved communities and townships. TMWF provides affordable broadband connectivity to households and small businesses in areas typically excluded from fibre rollout. This investment directly supports digital inclusion and connectivity access.
notes: F366001-01: $4.43M commit Dec 2024. Tier 1 keyword: broadband, connectivity, digital inclusion.

financier_name: British International Investment (BII, formerly CDC Group)
title: TymeBank / Tyme Group — Equity Investments (Digital Bank)
description: Series of equity investments in TymeBank (Tyme Group Holdings PTE), South Africa's leading digital bank focused on the underbanked. TymeBank integrates physical and digital distribution via kiosks at Pick n Pay and Boxer supermarkets. BII rounds: $18.5M equity (Nov 2021, directly in TymeBank Ltd — subsequently restructured/exited as separate entity), $18.8M equity in Tyme Group Holdings (Sep 2022), $5M equity follow-on (Nov 2022), $2.5M equity (Apr 2023), $1.1M equity (Jul 2024). TymeBank also operates GoTyme in Philippines.
co_financiers: JG Summit Holdings; Tencent; African Rainbow Capital; Norwest Venture Partners; British Patient Capital
notes: Complex restructuring: F336101-01 $18.5M (2021, TymeBank Ltd) → F336101-02 $-18.5M (2022, exit from TymeBank Ltd entity). Simultaneously new investment in Tyme Group Holdings (parent): F342001-01 $18.8M (Sep 2022), F342001-02 $5M (Nov 2022), F346101-03 $2.5M (Apr 2023), F356901-04 $1.1M (Jul 2024). Net active commitment = $27.4M in Tyme Group Holdings. Excluding F336101 (closed/restructured) from commit-usd. Total commit-usd is Tyme Group Holdings rounds only.

title: CPDIF Acquisition of Ctrack Africa and Middle East Operations from Inseego Corp
description: Convergence Partners Digital Infrastructure Fund (CPDIF) acquired 100% of Ctrack's operations in Africa and the Middle East from Nasdaq-listed Inseego Corp in a transaction valued at approximately ZAR 529 million (~US$36.2 million). This was CPDIF's maiden investment. Ctrack is a leading telematics software-as-a-service (SaaS) platform providing fleet management analytics, insurance risk solutions, and asset tracking solutions across sub-Saharan Africa. Ctrack's IoT and big data analytics platform leverages cloud-based data infrastructure, AI-driven analytics, and IoT connectivity. Headquartered in South Africa. In December 2024, Ctrack additionally acquired Inseego's international telematics business for $52 million.
cat_primary_name: Other Emerging Technologies (IoT, Quantum, Blockchain, etc.)
notes: Inseego SEC announcement Feb 2021 stated price ~ZAR529M (~$36.2M at ZAR/USD 14.62). Transaction closed August 2021 per Ctrack announcement. FX: IMF 2021 annual average ZAR/USD ~0.0682 → $36.2M (using deal-disclosed rate of 14.62). CPDIF Fund III maiden investment. Ctrack South Africa operations only in this tranche; separate Inseego international acquisition completed Dec 2024 by Ctrack (not a CPDIF direct ticket — Ctrack corporate M&A). BII CPDIF portfolio confirms Ctrack Africa Holdings Proprietary Limited, South Africa, July 2021.

title: Africa Data Centres South Africa — Johannesburg JHB1/JHB2 and Cape Town CPT1/CPT2 Data Centres
description: ADC invested significantly in South Africa as its largest market, operating data centres in Johannesburg (JHB1 Midrand: 14.4MW; JHB2 Samrand: 10MW expanding to 20MW) and Cape Town (CPT1: 12MW expanding to 20MW with new halls; CPT2: 20MW). The first DFC disbursement of $83 million in August 2022 primarily supported South Africa expansion. In June 2024, RMB arranged a ZAR 2 billion (~$109.5 million) bespoke financing facility for ADC to accelerate South Africa hyperscale capacity by an additional 20MW. Combined South Africa capacity from JHB1, JHB2, CPT1, and CPT2 represents 64MW+, the largest ADC market. The July 2024 Cape Town CPT1 expansion added three new halls with 6MW additional IT load.
co_financiers: US DFC (first disbursement $83M Aug 2022 primarily for South Africa; full DFC facility $300M REG; dfc-reg-africa-data-centres-2020); RMB ZAR2bn ($109.5M) Jun 2024 local debt facility
notes: commit-usd=~$128.6M = all-source project cost $428.6M minus DFC $300M debt = ADC equity/own contribution to the DFC-supported 5-country programme. Approx as the exact ADC equity slice vs RMB debt is not separately disclosed. DFC first disbursement $83M Aug 2022 primarily ZAF. RMB ZAR2bn ($109.5M) Jun 2024 is debt for ZAF expansion only. CPT1 expansion opened Jul 2024. JHB1 14.4MW, JHB2 10MW (expanding to 20MW), CPT1 12MW (expanding to 20MW), CPT2 20MW. CROSS-REF: dfc-reg-africa-data-centres-2020 in us_dfc.csv records DFC loan.

title: DPI investment in Ukheshe International South Africa digital fintech enabler
description: Development Partners International (DPI), via African Development Partners III (ADP III), co-invests with Fireball Capital in Ukheshe International, a South African digital fintech enablement platform. Ukheshe provides white-label digital financial services infrastructure to banks, telecoms and fintechs including digital wallets, payment APIs, virtual cards, and remittance solutions. Ukheshe subsequently acquired Masterpass (South Africa) in 2022 and EFT Corporation in 2024, expanding its digital payments rails footprint across southern Africa.
co_financiers: Fireball Capital
notes: Investment amount not publicly disclosed in press release. DPI invests via ADP III; Fireball Capital co-invests. Estimated USD 10M based on DPI's typical ticket range (USD 40-120M per deal disclosures, but Ukheshe is a smaller growth-stage company so lower estimate used). Amount-quality=Estimated. DPI's portfolio page lists Ukheshe under active investments. Ukheshe acquired Masterpass SA (2022) and EFT Corporation (2024) post-investment.

title: Visa South Africa Data Centre and Digital Infrastructure Investment
description: Visa opened its first African data centre in Johannesburg, South Africa in July 2025. The data centre — Visa's fifth globally — is part of a R1 billion (~$54–57 million) investment in South Africa over three years (2023–2025+). It localises processing of South African transactions on VisaNet and forms part of Visa's broader $1 billion Africa digital transformation pledge made in 2022. Investment also covers SME enablement, youth training and merchant digitisation.
notes: R1 billion ZAR converted at ZAR/USD 17.56 (Reuters conversion ~$57M). TechCabal clarification: R1B covers data centre plus other projects (not data centre alone). Exact CapEx breakdown undisclosed. commit-year set to 2023 (when investment work began per ITWeb: 'a project we've been running over the last two years'). Data centre went live July 2025. Part of wider $1B Africa commitment (visa-reg-1b-africa-commitment-2022).

title: Africa Data Centres South Africa — 20MW Hyperscale Expansion (RMB ZAR2bn Facility, 2024)
description: In June 2024, Africa Data Centres secured a ZAR 2 billion (~US$109.5 million) bespoke financing facility arranged by RMB (Rand Merchant Bank, part of FirstRand Group) to expand hyperscale data centre capacity in South Africa by an additional 20MW. RMB acted as Coordinator, Initial Mandated Lead Arranger, and Bookrunner. The financing enables ADC to accelerate expansion at its existing Midrand (JHB1) and Samrand (JHB2) Johannesburg campuses and Cape Town (CPT1/CPT2) facilities. ADC's South Africa market has capacity of 64MW and the expansion aims to increase this further to serve growing hyperscaler and enterprise demand.
co_financiers: RMB (Rand Merchant Bank / FirstRand) ZAR2bn debt facility — arranger of financing
notes: ZAR2bn ($109.5M at 2024 ZAR/USD ~18.25) arranged by RMB Jun 2024. ADC's own corporate commitment = the capex investment enabled by this debt facility (20MW expansion in ZAF). co-finance-usd=$109.5M represents the RMB debt component. Amount-quality=Approx (USD conversion estimated). Source: RMB press release 18 Jun 2024 and LaunchBase Africa Dec 2024. Separate from the DFC $300M master facility which covers multi-country. This is purely South Africa domestic financing.

recip_org: Independent Communications Authority of South Africa (ICASA) / Vodacom South Africa
title: Vodacom South Africa 5G/4G Spectrum Acquisition — 2022 ICASA Auction
description: Vodacom South Africa acquired 110 MHz of high-demand radio frequency spectrum in the ICASA multi-band spectrum auction concluded 17 March 2022 — the largest spectrum purchase in the auction. Portfolio: 2x10MHz in the 700MHz band, 80MHz in the 2600MHz band, and 10MHz in the 3500MHz band, for a total of R5.38 billion. The spectrum enables acceleration of 4G coverage and 5G network rollout across South Africa and supports Vodacom's network performance and digital inclusion goals. Spectrum licence effective 1 July 2022 for a 20-year period. R3.2 billion was paid in H1 FY2023 (before July 2022); remaining R2.2 billion paid October 2023 (800MHz band cleared of interference).
notes: ICASA auction 10-17 March 2022. Vodacom paid R5.38bn (confirmed R5.4bn rounded in annual report). USD = R5.38bn / 15.07 (ZAR/USD March 2022 spot) = ~$357M. R3.2bn paid H1 FY23; R2.2bn paid Oct 2023. Spectrum period 20yr (end-year=2042). Vodacom's 110MHz was largest share in auction (vs MTN 100MHz). Source: Vodacom SENS 17 Mar 2022; FY22 results booklet.

recip_org: Microsoft Azure South Africa — data centre expansion and AI infrastructure
title: Microsoft South Africa — $329M Data Centre and AI Expansion 2026
description: Microsoft announced a $329 million investment in South Africa in April 2026 to expand data centres, boost infrastructure resilience, and develop AI skills. Covers land acquisition for future data centre expansion, power and water readiness improvements, and capacity increases at existing Johannesburg and Cape Town facilities. Described as building on an earlier $1.2 billion total commitment to South Africa.
notes: $329M announced April 2026. Commit-year=2026. Separate from prior ZAR 20.4B (2019-2024) and ZAR 5.4B (2025-2027) rows. The '$1.2 billion prior commitment' cited in press = approximate cumulative of ZAR 20.4B rows.

title: AWS Africa — South Africa Cloud and AI Infrastructure Expansion by 2029 (2025 tranche)
description: AWS announced at the AWS Summit Johannesburg (September 2024) plans to invest an additional ZAR 30.4 billion (approximately USD 1.7 billion) in South Africa cloud and AI infrastructure by 2029, building on ZAR 15.6B already invested 2018-2022. financier_name: Industrial and Commercial Bank of China (ICBC)
title: ICBC provides $240 million loan to Cell C for second Frame Supply Agreement (FSA2) (Linked to Project ID#58622)
description: On March 30, 2015, Cell C Limited (or “Cell C”), a South African telecommunications company, entered into a second Frame Supply Agreement (FSA2) with Huawei relating to the supply of RAN equipment and the upgrade and expansion of their LTE, MBT, and microwave networks. In the same year, Cell C entered into a Capex loan facility of $240 million and ZAR 1.3 billion with the Industrial and Commercial Bank of China (ICBC) to finance this FSA2 (see Project ID#58622 for ZAR portion of financing). According to a 2017 circular to stockholders, Blue Label Telecom (majority stakeholder of Cell C) listed the current value of the loan at $95.473 million (2015: $44.446 million, 2014: $0 million) with an undrawn facility of $144.527 million. The loan bears an interest of Libor + 3.45%, which is accrued monthly and settled quarterly. A percentage of the capital outstanding is repayable annually based on a sliding scale. The balance includes $0.399 million accrued interest, and the loan reaches maturity on 30 June 2023. The loan is 50% guaranteed by Oger Telecom Limited.
notes: AidData staff note: 1. The month in which the loan was contracted is unknown, so the 6 month average Libor rate for 2015

title: DEG Equity Investment in Lulalend (Lula) Digital SME Lender South Africa (2023)
description: DEG participates in the USD 35 million Series B funding round for Lulalend (rebranded Lula), South Africa's leading digital lender providing working capital and financial services to small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). The round was led by Lightrock with DEG, Triodos Investment Management, Women's World Banking, IFC, and Quona Capital participating. Proceeds service surging demand for fast access to working capital and accelerate the rollout of Lula's digital banking offering for SMEs. The company was launched in 2014 and uses digital technology to assess creditworthiness and disburse loans rapidly.
co_financiers: Lightrock (lead); Triodos Investment Management; Women's World Banking; IFC; Quona Capital
notes: DEG confirmed as participant in USD 35M Series B (Lightrock-led, February 2023) per multiple press reports and Lulalend blog post. DEG-specific amount not publicly stated. USD 5,000,000 is an estimate; full round USD 35M split among 5+ investors. amount-quality=Estimated. ZAF (South Africa only - single country).

recip_org: Microsoft Azure South Africa — AI and cloud expansion, second region Centurion
title: Microsoft Azure South Africa — ZAR 5.4B AI and Cloud Expansion 2025-2027
description: Microsoft announced ZAR 5.4 billion (approximately USD 295 million) to expand cloud and AI infrastructure in South Africa by end of 2027, announced by President Brad Smith on March 6, 2025 alongside President Cyril Ramaphosa. Includes construction of a second cloud infrastructure region in Centurion, expanded data centre capacity, power and water readiness, and AI skills development for government and private sector. Builds on ZAR 20.4B invested 2019-2024.
notes: ZAR 5.4B new commitment announced March 2025 by Brad Smith. Reuters says ~$295M. USD 300M also cited (Forbes). Separate from prior ZAR 20.4B row. Window 2025-2027 explicitly named. Second SA cloud region being built in Centurion.

description: DEG provides USD 4 million in equity capital to South African insurtech Naked Insurance as part of its USD 38 million Series B2 funding round. title: DEG Equity Investment in Naked Insurtech South Africa Series B (2023)
description: DEG participates in the USD 17 million IFC-led equity round for Naked Insurance, South Africa's first fully digital insurance platform. The round is led by IFC with DEG, Hollard, and Yellowwoods as co-investors. Naked uses AI and automation to provide comprehensive and instant vehicle, home, contents, and standalone item insurance. A 2017 circular to stockholders from Blue Label Telecom (majority stakeholder of Cell C) listed the current value of the loan at R43.051 million (2015: R0 million, 2014: R0 million) with an undrawn facility of R1 256.949 million. The loan bears an interest of JIBAR plus a 3.45% margin, which is accrued monthly and settled quarterly. A percentage of the capital outstanding is repayable annually based on a sliding scale; the balance includes R0.421 million accrued interest. The loan's final  maturity date is June 30, 2023 and it is 50% guaranteed by Oger Telecom Limited.

title: Huawei Cloud South Africa — Multi-year cloud infrastructure CapEx (Johannesburg Availability Zones 1–3)
description: Huawei launched its first availability zone (AZ) in South Africa in November 2018/early 2019, becoming the first international cloud service provider with a hyperscale data centre in Africa. title: China Eximbank provides $65.4 million loan for Phase I of Smart Zambia National ICT Development Project (Linked to Project ID#92219)
description: In March 2015, the Government of Zambia and China Eximbank signed a concessional loan agreement worth $65,468,016.48 for Phase 1 of the Smart Zambia National ICT Development Project. The proceeds of the loan were used to be used by the borrower to finance 100% of the cost of a $65,468,016.48 commercial contract between the Government of Zambia and Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd. (Contract #00Y8941213000A), which was signed in January 2015. The contract was to be paid in three installments: one advance payment of 30% of the contract value, one payment worth 50% of the contract value at delivery, and a final payment equivalent to 20% of the contract value as an acceptance payment upon completion.   Phase 1 of Smart Zambia National ICT Development Project supported the creation of three data centers and an information and communications technology (ICT) talent training center (Chinese project name: 信息和通信技术人才培训中心). Zambia’s main national data center, managed by the Zambia Information and Communication Technology Authority (ZICTA), is located in Lusaka (exact locational coordinates: -15.423211184181552, 28.311501916335335). The ZICTA center covers an area about 450 square meters, with an equipment room containing 72 server cabinets, a power room, monitoring room, and two outdoor diesel-engine generators supported by underground fuel tanks. The ZICTA cloud platform was designed to provide processing, networking, and storage facilities for government and public institutions, and commercial enterprise services. The second data center is a a 400-square-meter backup facility located at the Roma switch office of Zamtel, the Zambian national telecommunications operator. The third data center is another 600-square meter backup facility situated at the Zamtel Kitwe switch office. All of the three centers are fully equipped with servers, power, monitoring, and communications equipment rooms.   Project implementation commenced in January 2016. title: China Eximbank provides $333.2 million preferential buyer’s credit for Phase II of Smart Zambia National ICT Development Project (Linked to Project ID#53093)
description: On December 28, 2017, China Eximbank and the Government of Zambia signed a $333,200,000 preferential buyer’s credit (PBC) agreement for Phase II of the Smart Zambia National ICT Development Project. The borrowing terms of the PBC are unknown. However, it is known that the borrower was expected to on-lend the proceeds of the PBC to ZESCO Limited, which would in turn used the proceeds to finance 85% of the cost of a $392 million commercial contract between the Government of Zambia (represented by the Cabinet Office) and Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd. As of July 2022, the loan from China Eximbank had achieved a 0% disbursement rate ($0 out of $333,200,000).   The purpose of Phase II of the Smart Zambia National ICT Development Project is to (a) extend the ZESCO national optical fiber backbone by 2,700 kilometers covering 64 backbone sites; (b) establish a metropolitan area network with a cumulative cable distance of 3,600 kilometers across 17 districts; and (c) implement a full IP-based Access Network to cover 2,750 kilometers covering 17 districts. Smart Zambia Institute, ZESCO Limited, and Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd. are all involved in project implementation.   Phase II commenced in May 2017; however, this has been the subject of local controversy. In early 2022, Dr, Mbita Chitala, the Former Chairman of ZESCO’s Board of Directors, published a book entitled “Corporate Capture, the Political Economy of Electricity Management in Zambia 2014-2021, (How Not to Manage a State Enterprise),” in which he claims that Phase II of the Smart Zambia National ICT Development Project is plagued by corruption. More specifically, he claims that Zambia’s Secretary to the Cabinet Dr. Roland Msiska and later Dr. Simon Miri and his deputy, former Deputy Finance minister and Bank of Zambia Governor Patrick Mvunga, placed extraordinary pressure on ZESCO to accept an on-lending agreement for Phase II of the Smart Zambia National ICT Development Project. title: China Development Bank provides $178.5 million loan for Safe City Project
description: In January 2013, Government of Zambia’s Ministry of Home Affairs and ZTE Corporation signed a $210 million commercial contract for the Safe City Project (also known as Phase III of the Public Security Network Project). Then, in September 2013, the then Minister of Home Affairs Edgar Lungu was forced to cancel the $210 million tender awarded to ZTE Corporation to install closed circuit television (CCTV) cameras on the streets of Lusaka after public outcry and allegations of corruption in the bidding process (including potential kickbacks to Patriotic Front officials). Lungu the referred the case to the Anti-Corruption Commission and the Auditor General at the Ministry of Justice for further investigation. Then, on April 14, 2015, Zambia’s Deputy Minister of Home Affairs Colonel Gerry Chanda announced that the Government of Zambia decided to ‘re-table’ the bid. Nearly 18 months later, in October 2016, China Development Bank (CDB) and the Government of Zambia signed a $178.5 million loan agreement for the Safe City Project. The borrowing terms of the loan are unknown. However, it is known that the borrower was expected to use the proceeds of the loan to partially finance a commercial contract between the Government of Zambia’s Ministry of Home Affairs and ZTE Corporation, which was signed in 2016.   The purpose of this project is to install CCTV cameras in various public places — such as streets, highways and bus stations — to enhance the monitoring of events within the city of Lusaka in order to curb crime. ZTE is the general (EPC) contractor responsible for project implementation. Construction started in December 2017.  However, according to a report from the Government of Zambia's Parliamentary Committee on National Security and Foreign Affairs, this project was only in the initial implementation stages as of June 2019. title: China Eximbank provides $302 government concessional loan for Phase 2 of the Communication Towers Project
description: In April 2016, the Government of Zambia and Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd. signed an MOU on Phase 2 of the Communication Towers Project. Then, on August 4, 2017, the Chinese Government and the Government of Zambia signed a preferential loan framework agreement for Phase 2 of the Communication Towers Project. On the same day, China Eximbank and the Government of the Republic of Zambia also signed a $302,034,321.68 government concessional loan (GCL) agreement for Phase II of the Smart Zambia National ICT Development Project. The borrowing terms of the GCL are unknown. However, it is known that the borrower was expected to use the proceeds of the loan to finance 100% of the cost of a commercial contract between the Government of Zambia and Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd., which was signed in August 2016. According to Ministry of Transport and Communications of Zambia, by the fourth quarter of 2018, the loan had achieved an 30.3% disbursement rate ($91,637,435.75 out of $302,034,321.68). As of July 2022, the Ministry of Finance & National Planning (MOFNP) of Zambia reported that the loan had achieved an 87.8% disbursement rate ($265,379,267.74 out of $302,034,321.68).   Phase 2 involved the construction of 808 communication towers, 1,009 2G, 3G and 4G wireless stations, as well as the installation of a matched transmission network and user access terminals in unserved and under-served areas. The broad objective is to improve communication services and reach 92 percent network coverage countrywide. Another objective is to ensure that 40 percent of the country has access to data services (from a baseline level of 5 percent). The targets of these communication towers will be relatively population dense villages and economically active areas like hospitals, schools, mines, and tourist areas. This is in line with the country's goal to provide universal access to information via information and communications technology (ICT). Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd. is the contractor resp

title: Chinese Government provides RMB 33 million interest-free loan for Phase 1 of High-Performance Computing Center Project (Linked to Project ID #62405, #62408)
description: In 2015, the Chinese Government provided an RMB 33 million interest-free loan for Phase 1 of the High Performance Computing Center. This loan was originally intended for the 2015-2017 timeframe but was not actually distributed for unspecified reasons. Then, on April 3, 2018 during President Emmerson Mnangawa's visit to China, the two sides signed bilateral cooperation agreements in the fields of economy and technology, agriculture, science and technology, and human resources development. At the time of signing, Zimbabwe was still processing paperwork related to the RMB 33 million transaction from 2015.   This project involved the installation of a supercomputer facility (high-performance computing center) at the University of Zimbabwe. The facility was expected to be used in agriculture, weather forecast, mining, gene technology, and stimulation, and support scientific research in Zimbabwe. The project also involved the training of dozens of Zimbabwean researchers on how to operate the supercomputer.   Inspur Group — China's leading personal computer and server manufacturer — and Dameng were both involved in the implementation of the project.  On February 6, 2015, the project was officially completed and and handed over to the University of Zimbabwe.   In 2017, the Chinese Government supported Phase 2 of this project with an RMB 100 million grant (see Project ID #62405). Another Chinese Government grant was provided in 2018 (see Project ID #62408).

title: China Eximbank provides RMB 485 million government concessional loan for Phase 3 of NetOne Expansion Project (Linked to Project ID#62426, #62416, #62417)
description: On June 1, 2010, China Eximbank and the Government of Zimbabwe signed an RMB 315 million government concessional loan (GCL) agreement for Phase 1 of the NetOne Expansion Project (captured via Project ID#62416). Zimbabwe’s President Robert Mugabe and Wang Gang, a member of the Political Bureau of the CPC Central Committee, witnessed the signing of the loan agreement. The Phase 1 GCL carried the following borrowing terms: a 20 year maturity and a 5 year grace period. However, according to the the 2018 and 2019 Blue Book Estimates of Revenue and Expenditure published by Zimbabwe’s Ministry of Finance and Economic Development, its face value was later revised to RMB 286,080,176.66. The Phase 1 GCL’s amount outstanding (including principal, interest, and arrears) was $42,068,995.10 as of 2021. The Chinese Government also provided an RMB 50 million grant for Phase 1, which is captured via Project ID#62417.    The purpose of Phase 1 was to facilitate the country’s transition to second generation (2G) and third generation (3G) phone technology by expanding the coverage and capacity of NetOne’s network and more than doubling its subscriber base. NetOne Zimbabwe is a company that is wholly owned by the Zimbabwean government. It is one of three mobile phone operators in the country. It used the proceeds of the Phase 1 GCL to purchase equipment from Huawei Technologies and implement network upgrades to wireless broadband stations, while expanding coverage to remote geographic areas lacking service. Its ultimate goal was to reach 7 million subscribers. During Phase 1, a Broadband and Ascot Packet Core Switching Centre in Bulawayo was completed and officially inaugurated on December 7, 2011. By 2012, NetOne had successfully launched mobile broadband and 3G service in Zimbabwe. The geographical areas of the country that received upgraded internet service during Phase 1 included the Southern Region, Matabeleland Province, and Midlands Province. title: Chinese Government provides RMB 100 million grant for Phase 2 of High-Performance Computing Center Project (linked to projects #62320, #62408)
description: On December 6, 2017, the Chinese Government and the Government of Zimbabwe signed an RMB 100 million grant agreement for Phase 2 of the High-Performance Computing Center Project.  This purpose of this project was to increase the computational capacity of the supercomputer facility (high-performance computing center) at the University of Zimbabwe by nearly 10 times from 36 teraflops per second to over 300.  The project also involves the introduction of new technologies, such as cloud services and cloud computing. title: China Eximbank provides $98.6 million preferential buyer’s credit for Phase 2 of TelOne Broadband Expansion Project
description: On December 1, 2015, China Eximbank and the Government of Zimbabwe signed a $98,617,482 preferential buyer’s credit (PBC) agreement for Phase 2 of the TelOne Broadband Expansion Project. Then, on April 5, 2016, Zimbabwe’s Parliament ratified the PBC agreement. The PBC carries the following borrowing terms: a 2% interest rate, a 20-year maturity, a 5-year grace period, a 0.25% commitment fee, and a 0.25% management fee. The Government of Zimbabwe, in turn, used the proceeds of the PBC to on-lend to TelOne, a state-owned telecommunications company at a 5% interest rate. The ultimate borrower (TelOne) was expected to use the loan proceeds to finance 85% of the cost of its $116.8 million commercial contract with Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd. The remaining 15% was to be directly funded by TelOne. The guarantor of the loan is NetOne (Private) Limited.   The borrower is expected to maintain a minimum cash balance in an escrow account (debt service reserve account) at China Eximbank. The balance of the escrow account was $0 as of December 31, 2016, $4,341,790 as of December 31, 2017, $4,343,991 as of December 31, 2018, ZWL 220,015,319 as of December 31, 2019, ZWL 157,681,155 as of December 31, 2020, ZWL 720,123,439 as of December 31, 2021, and ZWL 1,294,710,690 as of December 31, 2022. In the event that the funds in the escrow account are insufficient to repay the interest and the principal of the loan, the PBC agreement indicates that the extra revenues from an escrow account that was set up for another China Eximbank-financed project (Phase 3 of NetOne Expansion Project, which is captured in Project ID#52620) will be used to settle the TelOne loan.   The borrower is responsible for making 30, equal semiannual repayments between January 21, 2022 and January 21, 2037. The first drawdown (disbursement) took place on March 14, 2017. notes: AidData staff note: 1. This project is also known as Tel-One Backbone and Broadband Access Phase Two Project, the TelOne
